infobox.key: City and County of San Francisco,
infobox.value: is the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_California_cities_by_population "List of California cities by population") in [California](/wiki/California "California") and the [17th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), with a population of 826,079 in 2024.[[12]](#cite_note-Quickfacts_2025-12) Among U.S. cities with a population of 300,000 or more, San Francisco is ranked first by per capita income, [second by population density](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density "List of United States cities by population density"), and sixth by aggregate income as of 2023.[[25]](#cite_note-ipums_nhgis-27) Some 4.6 million residents live in the city's metropolitan statistical area, which is the [13th-largest](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the United States. Around 9.2 million live in the [San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland combined statistical area](/wiki/San_Jose%E2%80%93San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_combined_statistical_area "San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland combined statistical area"), the [fifth-largest](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") in the United States.

**San Francisco**,[[a]](#cite_note-26) officially the **City and County of San Francisco,** is the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_California_cities_by_population "List of California cities by population") in [California](/wiki/California "California") and the [17th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), with a population of 826,079 in 2024.[[12]](#cite_note-Quickfacts_2025-12) Among U.S. cities with a population of 300,000 or more, San Francisco is ranked first by per capita income, [second by population density](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density "List of United States cities by population density"), and sixth by aggregate income as of 2023.[[25]](#cite_note-ipums_nhgis-27) Some 4.6 million residents live in the city's metropolitan statistical area, which is the [13th-largest](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the United States. Around 9.2 million live in the [San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland combined statistical area](/wiki/San_Jose%E2%80%93San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_combined_statistical_area "San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland combined statistical area"), the [fifth-largest](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") in the United States.

Prior to [European settlement](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas"), San Francisco was inhabited by the [Yelamu Ohlone](/wiki/Yelamu "Yelamu"). On June 29, 1776, [settlers from New Spain](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") established the [Presidio of San Francisco](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") at the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate"), and the [Mission San Francisco de Asís](/wiki/Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs "Mission San Francisco de Asís") a few miles away, both named for [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi").[[1]](#cite_note-DeAnza-Moraga-Palou-1) The [California gold rush](/wiki/California_gold_rush "California gold rush") of 1849 brought rapid growth, making it the largest city on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") at the time.[[25]](#cite_note-ipums_nhgis-27) In 1856, San Francisco became a [consolidated city-county](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county").[[26]](#cite_note-Coy_1919_409-28) After three-quarters of the city was destroyed by the [1906 earthquake and fire](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake"),[[27]](#cite_note-Montagne-29) it was quickly rebuilt, hosting the [Panama–Pacific International Exposition](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93Pacific_International_Exposition "Panama–Pacific International Exposition") nine years later. In [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was a major port of embarkation for [naval service](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy") members shipping out to the [Pacific Theater](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean_theater_of_World_War_II "Pacific Ocean theater of World War II").[[28]](#cite_note-WWII-30) After the war, the confluence of returning servicemen, significant [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States"), liberalizing attitudes, the rise of the [beatnik](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik") and [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") countercultures, the [sexual revolution](/wiki/Sexual_revolution "Sexual revolution"), [opposition to U.S. involvement in the Vietnam War](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War"), and other factors led to the [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love") and the [gay rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ rights in the United States") movement, cementing San Francisco as a center of [liberal activism](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States").

The [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area") is among the most economically productive large urban areas in the United States and the world, with a GDP of $1.332 trillion in 2024, [higher than all but 17 countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"). As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), the OECD-defined metropolitan area, with 6.7 million residents, ranked 5th by GDP ($874 billion) and 2nd by GDP per capita ($131,082) across all metropolitan areas in the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) In 2024, San Francisco proper had a GDP of $268 billion and a GDP per capita of $324,000.[[32]](#cite_note-:1-34) This productivity is spurred by its [leading universities](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_San_Francisco "List of colleges and universities in San Francisco"),[[33]](#cite_note-35) [high-tech](/wiki/IT "IT"), [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare_industry "Healthcare industry"), [finance](/wiki/Finance_industry "Finance industry"), [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance"), [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate_industry "Real estate industry"), and professional services sectors.[[34]](#cite_note-36) The city is home to [numerous companies](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_San_Francisco "List of companies based in San Francisco")—many in the [technology sector](/wiki/Technology_company "Technology company")—including [Salesforce](/wiki/Salesforce.com "Salesforce.com"), [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber"), [Airbnb](/wiki/Airbnb "Airbnb"), [Levi's](/wiki/Levi_Strauss_%26_Co. "Levi Strauss & Co."), [Gap](/wiki/Gap_Inc. "Gap Inc."), [OpenAI](/wiki/OpenAI "OpenAI"), [Chime](/wiki/Chime_(company) "Chime (company)"), [Anthropic](/wiki/Anthropic "Anthropic"), [Databricks](/wiki/Databricks "Databricks"), [Dropbox](/wiki/Dropbox "Dropbox"), and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft").

In 2022, San Francisco had more than 1.7 million international visitors and approximately 20 million domestic visitors.[[35]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38) It is known for its steep rolling [hills](/wiki/List_of_hills_in_San_Francisco "List of hills in San Francisco") and [eclectic mix of architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_San_Francisco "Architecture of San Francisco") across [varied neighborhoods](/wiki/San_Francisco_neighborhoods "San Francisco neighborhoods"); its [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") and [Mission](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco") districts; mild climate; and landmarks including the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge"), [cable cars](/wiki/San_Francisco_cable_car_system "San Francisco cable car system"), and [Alcatraz](/wiki/Alcatraz_Federal_Penitentiary "Alcatraz Federal Penitentiary").[[37]](#cite_note-39) The city is home to educational and cultural institutions such as the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco"), the [University of San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_San_Francisco "University of San Francisco"), [San Francisco State University](/wiki/San_Francisco_State_University "San Francisco State University"), the [San Francisco Conservatory of Music](/wiki/San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music "San Francisco Conservatory of Music"), the [Legion of Honor](/wiki/Legion_of_Honor_(museum) "Legion of Honor (museum)"), the [de Young Museum](/wiki/De_Young_Museum "De Young Museum"), the [San Francisco Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/San_Francisco_Museum_of_Modern_Art "San Francisco Museum of Modern Art"), the [San Francisco Symphony](/wiki/San_Francisco_Symphony "San Francisco Symphony"), the [San Francisco Ballet](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ballet "San Francisco Ballet"), the [San Francisco Opera](/wiki/San_Francisco_Opera "San Francisco Opera"), the [SFJAZZ Center](/wiki/SFJAZZ_Center "SFJAZZ Center"), and the [California Academy of Sciences](/wiki/California_Academy_of_Sciences "California Academy of Sciences"). Two [major league](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") sports teams, the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") and the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors"), play their home games within San Francisco. [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport") (SFO) is one of the world's busiest airports, while [a light rail and bus network](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Railway "San Francisco Municipal Railway"), in tandem with the [BART](/wiki/Bay_Area_Rapid_Transit "Bay Area Rapid Transit") and [Caltrain](/wiki/Caltrain "Caltrain") systems, connects nearly every part of San Francisco with the wider region.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

See also: [List of San Francisco placename etymologies](/wiki/List_of_San_Francisco_placename_etymologies "List of San Francisco placename etymologies")

San Francisco, which is Spanish for "[Saint Francis](/wiki/Saint_Francis "Saint Francis")", takes its name from [Mission San Francisco de Asís](/wiki/Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs "Mission San Francisco de Asís"), which in turn was named after [Saint Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi").[[40]](#cite_note-42) The mission received its name in 1776, when it was founded by the Spanish under the leadership of Padre [Francisco Palóu](/wiki/Francisco_Pal%C3%B3u "Francisco Palóu"). The city has officially been known as San Francisco since 1847, when [Washington Allon Bartlett](/wiki/Washington_Allon_Bartlett "Washington Allon Bartlett"), then serving as [the city's alcalde](/wiki/List_of_pre-statehood_mayors_of_San_Francisco "List of pre-statehood mayors of San Francisco"), renamed it from [Yerba Buena](/wiki/Yerba_Buena,_California "Yerba Buena, California") (Spanish for "Good Herb"), which had been the name of the first civilian *pueblo* in San Francisco. Earlier in San Francisco's history, the uninhabited area on the northeastern side of San Francisco was called *El Paraje de Yerba Buena*, after the herb that was growing abundantly there. The name Yerba Buena continues to be used in locations in the city, such as on [Yerba Buena Island](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Island "Yerba Buena Island").

When using a nickname or abbreviation, local residents most commonly refer to San Francisco as "the City" or "SF."[[23]](#cite_note-TBI20130630-24)[[41]](#cite_note-is-it-ever-ok-43) Although the nickname "Frisco" has a local pedigree dating at least to 1850 and has been used by some local residents in every generation since then, the uses of both "Frisco" and the historically more recent "San Fran" tend to elicit sharp divisions among residents.[[41]](#cite_note-is-it-ever-ok-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45)

The [California Cantonese](/wiki/California_Cantonese "California Cantonese") who came for the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") named California and specifically San Francisco 金山, *Gāmsāan*, "[Gold Mountain](/wiki/Gold_Mountain_(toponym) "Gold Mountain (toponym)")".
When gold was discovered in [Bendigo](/wiki/Bendigo "Bendigo"), Victoria, Australia, it was named "New Gold Mountain" ([Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese_language "Yue Chinese language"): 新金山, *Sān Gāmsāan*) and California and San Francisco as "Old Gold Mountain" (舊金山, *Gāu Gāmsāan*).[[44]](#cite_note-Yip-46)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of San Francisco](/wiki/History_of_San_Francisco "History of San Francisco")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of San Francisco](/wiki/Timeline_of_San_Francisco "Timeline of San Francisco").

### Indigenous history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Indigenous history")]

The earliest [archeological evidence](/wiki/Archeological_site "Archeological site") of [human habitation](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") in the area dates to 3000 BCE.[[45]](#cite_note-47) The [Ohlone](/wiki/Ohlone_languages "Ohlone languages") name for San Francisco was *Ahwaste*, meaning "place at the bay".[[46]](#cite_note-48) The [Yelamu](/wiki/Yelamu "Yelamu") group of the [Ramaytush](/wiki/Ramaytush "Ramaytush") people lived in a few small villages when an overland [Spanish exploration party](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") arrived on November 2, 1769, the first documented European visit to [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay").[[47]](#cite_note-Portola-49) The arrival of Spanish colonists, and the establishment of the Mission system, marked the beginning of the assimilation of the Ramaytush people and the decline of their language and culture.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### Spanish era (1769–1821)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spanish era (1769–1821)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Victor_Adam_after_Louis_Choris_-_Vue_du_Presidio_san_Francisco%2C_1822_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Victor_Adam_after_Louis_Choris_-_Vue_du_Presidio_san_Francisco%2C_1822_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victor_Adam_after_Louis_Choris_-_Vue_du_Presidio_san_Francisco,_1822_(cropped).jpg)

[Juan Bautista de Anza](/wiki/Juan_Bautista_de_Anza "Juan Bautista de Anza") established the [Presidio of San Francisco](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") for the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1776.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs_%28Oriana_Day%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs_%28Oriana_Day%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs_(Oriana_Day)_(cropped).jpg)

[Mission San Francisco de Asís](/wiki/Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs "Mission San Francisco de Asís") was founded by Padre [Francisco Palóu](/wiki/Francisco_Pal%C3%B3u "Francisco Palóu") on October 9, 1776.

The [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") claimed San Francisco as part of [Las Californias](/wiki/Las_Californias "Las Californias"), a province of the [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain"). The Spanish first arrived in what is now San Francisco on November 2, 1769, when the [Portolá expedition](/wiki/Portol%C3%A1_expedition "Portolá expedition"), led by Don [Gaspar de Portolá](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Portol%C3%A1 "Gaspar de Portolá") and [Juan Crespí](/wiki/Juan_Cresp%C3%AD "Juan Crespí"), arrived at [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay"). Having noted the strategic benefits of the area due to its large natural harbor, the Spanish dispatched [Pedro Fages](/wiki/Pedro_Fages "Pedro Fages") in 1770 to find a more direct route to the [San Francisco Peninsula](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peninsula "San Francisco Peninsula") from [Monterey](/wiki/Monterey,_California "Monterey, California"), which would become part of the [El Camino Real](/wiki/El_Camino_Real_(California) "El Camino Real (California)") route.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

In 1774, [Juan Bautista de Anza](/wiki/Juan_Bautista_de_Anza "Juan Bautista de Anza") arrived in the area to select sites for a [mission](/wiki/Spanish_missions_in_California "Spanish missions in California") and [presidio](/wiki/Presidios_of_California "Presidios of California"). The first European maritime presence in San Francisco Bay occurred on August 5, 1775, when the Spanish ship [*San Carlos*](/wiki/San_Carlos_(1768_ship) "San Carlos (1768 ship)"), commanded by [Juan Manuel de Ayala](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_de_Ayala "Juan Manuel de Ayala"), became the first ship to anchor in the bay.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

Soon after, on March 28, 1776, Anza established the [Presidio of San Francisco](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") and on October 9, [Mission San Francisco de Asís](/wiki/Mission_San_Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs "Mission San Francisco de Asís"), also known as Mission Dolores, was founded by Padre [Francisco Palóu](/wiki/Francisco_Pal%C3%B3u "Francisco Palóu").[[1]](#cite_note-DeAnza-Moraga-Palou-1) In 1794, the Presidio established the [Castillo de San Joaquín](/wiki/Fort_Point_National_Historic_Site "Fort Point National Historic Site"), a fortification on the southern side of the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate"), which later came to be known as Fort Point.

In 1804, the province of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") was created, which included San Francisco. At its peak in 1810–1820, the average population at the Mission Dolores settlement was about 1,100 people.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

### Mexican era (1821–1848)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mexican era (1821–1848)")]

See also: [Yerba Buena, California](/wiki/Yerba_Buena,_California "Yerba Buena, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Do%C3%B1a_Juana_Briones_de_Miranda_%287222900876%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Do%C3%B1a_Juana_Briones_de_Miranda_%287222900876%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Do%C3%B1a_Juana_Briones_de_Miranda_(7222900876)_(cropped).jpg)

[Juana Briones de Miranda](/wiki/Juana_Briones_de_Miranda "Juana Briones de Miranda"), known as the "Founding Mother of San Francisco"[[54]](#cite_note-Briones_de_Miranda-56)

In 1821, the [Californias](/wiki/The_Californias "The Californias") were [ceded to Mexico](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence") by Spain. The extensive [California mission system](/wiki/Missions_in_California "Missions in California") gradually lost its influence during the period of [Mexican rule](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic"), although it was not until 1833 that the missions would be secularized. Agricultural land became largely privatized as [ranchos](/wiki/Ranchos_of_California "Ranchos of California"), as was occurring in other parts of California. Coastal trade increased, including a half-dozen [barques](/wiki/Barques "Barques") from various Atlantic ports which regularly sailed in California waters.[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum-early-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)

With the enactment of the [Mexican Secularization Act of 1833](/wiki/Mexican_Secularization_Act_of_1833 "Mexican Secularization Act of 1833"), the missions were required to divest themselves of their extensive landholdings and emancipate the indigenous people under their control. As part of the process of secularization, Governor [José Figueroa](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Figueroa "José Figueroa") opened up San Francisco to civilian settlement. Prior to secularization, the only settlements in San Francisco had been the military settlement at the Presidio and the religious settlement at Mission Dolores.[[54]](#cite_note-Briones_de_Miranda-56) In 1835, the Presidio garrison, commanded by [Mariano Vallejo](/wiki/Mariano_Vallejo "Mariano Vallejo"), relocated to the [Presidio of Sonoma](/wiki/Presidio_of_Sonoma "Presidio of Sonoma"), which was regarded as needing a greater military presence due to the proximity of the Russian settlement at [Fort Ross](/wiki/Fort_Ross "Fort Ross"). Only a small detachment remained at the Presidio of San Francisco.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Mission Dolores sold most of its property in 1836, retaining only the church and related structures.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/San_Francisco_in_July_1849_Painting_by_George_Henry_Burgess.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_in_July_1849_Painting_by_George_Henry_Burgess.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_in_July_1849_Painting_by_George_Henry_Burgess.jpg)

San Francisco in July 1849 Painting by George Henry Burgess

In 1833, [Juana Briones de Miranda](/wiki/Juana_Briones_de_Miranda "Juana Briones de Miranda") was granted a small [rancho](/wiki/Ranchos_of_California "Ranchos of California") at [El Polín Spring](/wiki/El_Pol%C3%ADn_Spring "El Polín Spring"), near the Presidio, founding the first non-indigenous civilian household in San Francisco.[[54]](#cite_note-Briones_de_Miranda-56) In 1834, the *pueblo* of [Yerba Buena](/wiki/Yerba_Buena,_California "Yerba Buena, California") was founded. [Yerba Buena Cove](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Cove "Yerba Buena Cove") (named after [a native herb](/wiki/Clinopodium_douglasii "Clinopodium douglasii")) was already a favored anchorage spot and the new settlement and trading post was founded by its shores. Yerba Buena was located in what is now the city's [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") and [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco") and was centered on a plaza that is now [Portsmouth Square](/wiki/Portsmouth_Square "Portsmouth Square").[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum-early-57)[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-browning-62) A land survey of Yerba Buena was made by the Swiss immigrant [Jean Jacques Vioget](/wiki/Jean_Jacques_Vioget "Jean Jacques Vioget") as prelude to the city plan.[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum-early-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/A_view_of_Yerba_Buena_%28San_Francisco%29._On_July_9%2C_1846%2C_Capt._Montgomery_of_the_Sloop_of_War_Portsmouth%2C_raised_the_American_Flag_in_the_plaza_and_seized_the_region_of_the_United_States_LCCN2004666425.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_view_of_Yerba_Buena_(San_Francisco)._On_July_9,_1846,_Capt._Montgomery_of_the_Sloop_of_War_Portsmouth,_raised_the_American_Flag_in_the_plaza_and_seized_the_region_of_the_United_States_LCCN2004666425.jpg)

The 1846 [Capture of Yerba Buena](/wiki/Capture_of_Yerba_Buena "Capture of Yerba Buena") was an early U.S. victory in the [American conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California").

[Francisco de Haro](/wiki/Francisco_de_Haro "Francisco de Haro") became the first [alcalde](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde") of Yerba Buena. In 1835, the second alcalde, [José Joaquín Estudillo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Joaqu%C3%ADn_Estudillo "José Joaquín Estudillo"), approved the first land grant in Yerba Buena: to [William Richardson](/wiki/William_A._Richardson "William A. Richardson").[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum-early-57) Yerba Buena began to attract American and European settlers; an 1842 census listed 21 residents (11%) born in the United States or Europe, as well as one Filipino merchant.[[61]](#cite_note-63)
Following the [Bear Flag Revolt](/wiki/Bear_Flag_Revolt "Bear Flag Revolt") in Sonoma and the beginning of the [U.S. Conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California"), American forces from the *[USS Portsmouth](/wiki/USS_Portsmouth_(1843) "USS Portsmouth (1843)")* under the command of [John B. Montgomery](/wiki/John_B._Montgomery "John B. Montgomery") captured Yerba Buena on July 9, 1846, with little resistance from the local Californio population, raising the American flag over Yerba Buena plaza (later renamed Portsmouth Square in commemoration of this event).

Following the capture, U.S. forces appointed both [José de Jesús Noé](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Jes%C3%BAs_No%C3%A9 "José de Jesús Noé") and [Washington Allon Bartlett](/wiki/Washington_Allon_Bartlett "Washington Allon Bartlett") to serve as co-[alcaldes](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde") (mayors), while the conquest continued on in the rest of California. On January 30, 1847, Mayor Bartlett ordained that the city should officially change its name from "Yerba Buena" to "San Francisco", as the former name was only locally recognized and the latter name was in use on international maps.[[62]](#cite_note-64) There was also concern that a new town being planned by General Vallejo on the [Carquinez Strait](/wiki/Carquinez_Strait "Carquinez Strait") was to be called "Francisca", after the first name of his wife. After the name change to "San Francisco", the name of Vallejo's town was changed to [Benicia](/wiki/Benicia,_California "Benicia, California"), after his wife's middle name. Following the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848, [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") was [ceded from Mexico to the United States](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession").

### Early American era (1848–1906)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early American era (1848–1906)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/SanFranciscoharbor1851c_sharp.jpg/250px-SanFranciscoharbor1851c_sharp.jpg)](/wiki/File:SanFranciscoharbor1851c_sharp.jpg)

[Port of San Francisco](/wiki/Port_of_San_Francisco "Port of San Francisco") in 1851

Despite its attractive location as a port and naval base, San Francisco under American sovereignty was still a small settlement with inhospitable geography, lacking most of the basic facilities for a proper settlement.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley20004–5-65) Its 1847 population was said to be 459.[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum-early-57)

The [California gold rush](/wiki/California_gold_rush "California gold rush") brought a flood of treasure seekers. Prospectors accumulated in San Francisco over rival [Benicia](/wiki/Benicia,_California "Benicia, California"),[[64]](#cite_note-66) raising the population from 1,000 in 1848 to 25,000 by December 1849.[[65]](#cite_note-1849pop-67) The promise of wealth was so strong that crews on arriving vessels deserted and rushed off to the gold fields, leaving behind a forest of [masts](/wiki/Mast_(sailing) "Mast (sailing)") in San Francisco harbor.[[66]](#cite_note-buriedships-68) Some of these approximately 500 abandoned ships were used at times as [storeships](/wiki/Storeship "Storeship"), [saloons](/wiki/Bar_(establishment) "Bar (establishment)"), and hotels; many were left to rot, and some were sunk to establish title to the underwater lot. By 1851, the harbor was extended out into the bay by wharves while buildings were erected on piles among the ships. By 1870, [Yerba Buena Cove](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Cove "Yerba Buena Cove") had been filled to create new land.[[67]](#cite_note-buriedshipsSFgenalogy-69) Buried ships are occasionally exposed when foundations are dug for new buildings.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

California was quickly [granted statehood](/wiki/Compromise_of_1850 "Compromise of 1850") in 1850, and the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") built [Fort Point](/wiki/Fort_Point,_San_Francisco "Fort Point, San Francisco") at the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate") and a fort on [Alcatraz Island](/wiki/Alcatraz_Island "Alcatraz Island") to secure the bay. San Francisco County was one of the state's 18 original counties established at California statehood in 1850.[[69]](#cite_note-71) San Francisco's city limits originally extended west to [Divisadero Street](/wiki/Divisadero_Street "Divisadero Street") and Castro Street, and south to 20th Street, but in 1856, the California state government divided the county. A straight line was then drawn across the tip of the San Francisco Peninsula just north of [San Bruno Mountain](/wiki/San_Bruno_Mountain "San Bruno Mountain"). Everything south of the line became the new San Mateo County, while everything north of the line became the new consolidated City and County of San Francisco.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Bank_of_California_Building_San_Francisco_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bank_of_California_Building_San_Francisco_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bank_of_California_Building_San_Francisco_(cropped).jpg)

The [Bank of California](/wiki/Bank_of_California "Bank of California"), established in 1864, was the first commercial bank in [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States").[[71]](#cite_note-73)

In addition to triggering a sharp growth in population, The California gold rush also triggered a wave of entrepreneurial activity as individuals sought to capitalize on the newfound wealth. The discovery of silver deposits, notably the [Comstock Lode](/wiki/Comstock_Lode "Comstock Lode") in Nevada in 1859, further fueled rapid population growth and economic expansion.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200031–33-74)

The influx of fortune seekers and surge in population also brought challenges, including heightened criminal activity and poverty. Lawlessness became rampant, and the [Barbary Coast](/wiki/Barbary_Coast,_San_Francisco "Barbary Coast, San Francisco") district became synonymous with vice, attracting criminals, prostitutes, and illicit activities, including but not limited to prostitution, [bootlegging](/wiki/Rum-running "Rum-running"), and gambling.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley2000237–238-75) The rapidly growing population, with its lawlessness, gambling and other vices, and the fact that there were no churches to be found, prompted missionaries like [William Taylor](/wiki/William_Taylor_(missionary) "William Taylor (missionary)") to come to San Francisco where he began preaching in the streets, using an upright barrel as his pulpit. Taylor garnered enough generous donations from successful gold miners to build a church.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1895195-76)

One of the most influential figures of this era was [William Chapman Ralston](/wiki/William_Chapman_Ralston "William Chapman Ralston"). A shrewd banker and investor, Ralston amassed considerable wealth and influence in San Francisco. He gained control over a significant portion of the [Comstock Lode](/wiki/Comstock_Lode "Comstock Lode")'s gold and silver mines, establishing a virtual monopoly. Using his incredible clout, Ralston was able to generate millions of dollars for San Francisco during its heady boom years.[[75]](#cite_note-77) He founded the [Bank of California](/wiki/Bank_of_California "Bank of California"), the first bank in the Western United States, and built the opulent Palace Hotel, a symbol of San Francisco's newfound prosperity and the largest hotel in the country at the time.[[76]](#cite_note-78) His financial empire, however, collapsed in 1875 as a result of the [Panic of 1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873"), triggering a major economic crisis in San Francisco.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/California_-_the_Chinese_agitation_in_San_Francisco_-_a_meeting_of_the_Workingmen%27s_Party_on_the_sand_lots_%28H.A._Rodgers%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-California_-_the_Chinese_agitation_in_San_Francisco_-_a_meeting_of_the_Workingmen%27s_Party_on_the_sand_lots_%28H.A._Rodgers%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:California_-_the_Chinese_agitation_in_San_Francisco_-_a_meeting_of_the_Workingmen%27s_Party_on_the_sand_lots_(H.A._Rodgers)_(cropped).jpg)

Illustration by H.A. Rodgers for *[Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper](/wiki/Frank_Leslie%27s_Illustrated_Newspaper "Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper")* (March 20, 1880) showing a [Workingmen's Party of California](/wiki/Workingmen%27s_Party_of_California "Workingmen's Party of California") anti-Chinese rally on the sand lots near [San Francisco City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall "San Francisco City Hall")

The resulting unemployment and poverty from the [Long Depression](/wiki/Long_Depression "Long Depression") led to a rise in [anti-Chinese sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Chinese_sentiment "Anti-Chinese sentiment") that culminated in the [San Francisco riot of 1877](/wiki/San_Francisco_riot_of_1877 "San Francisco riot of 1877"), in which a mob of the city's majority [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") population descended on [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco"), killing four and causing $100,000 worth of property damage.[[77]](#cite_note-79) These events gave rise to the [Workingmen's Party of California](/wiki/Workingmen%27s_Party_of_California "Workingmen's Party of California"),[[78]](#cite_note-kauer-80) led by [drayman](/wiki/Drayman "Drayman") [Denis Kearney](/wiki/Denis_Kearney "Denis Kearney") and mobilized by his slogan "The Chinese must go!"[[79]](#cite_note-81) The party elected one third of delegates to [California's Second Constitutional Convention](/wiki/California_Constitutional_Conventions#Sacramento_Convention_of_1878–79 "California Constitutional Conventions") (the most of any organized party),[[78]](#cite_note-kauer-80) rewriting the [state constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_California "Constitution of California") to deny [Chinese Americans](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans") voting rights in California.[[80]](#cite_note-82) This agitation paved the way for the [Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882](/wiki/Chinese_Exclusion_Act_of_1882 "Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882"), which prohibited all immigration of Chinese laborers to the United States until it was [repealed](/wiki/Chinese_Exclusion_Repeal_Act "Chinese Exclusion Repeal Act") over 60 years later.

Development of the [Port of San Francisco](/wiki/Port_of_San_Francisco "Port of San Francisco") and the establishment in 1869 of overland access to the eastern U.S. rail system via the newly completed [Pacific Railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") (the construction of which the city only reluctantly helped support)[[b]](#cite_note-83) helped make the Bay Area a center for trade. Catering to the needs and tastes of the growing population, [Levi Strauss](/wiki/Levi_Strauss "Levi Strauss") opened a [dry goods](/wiki/Dry_goods "Dry goods") business and [Domingo Ghirardelli](/wiki/Domingo_Ghirardelli "Domingo Ghirardelli") began manufacturing chocolate. Chinese immigrants made the city a polyglot culture, drawn to "Old Gold Mountain", creating the city's [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") quarter. By 1880, Chinese made up 9.3% of the population.[[81]](#cite_note-IPUMSUSA-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/The_City_of_San_Francisco%2C_panorama_by_Currier_%26_Ives%2C_1878_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-The_City_of_San_Francisco%2C_panorama_by_Currier_%26_Ives%2C_1878_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_City_of_San_Francisco,_panorama_by_Currier_%26_Ives,_1878_(cropped).jpg)

A view of the city in 1878

The first [cable cars](/wiki/San_Francisco_cable_car_system "San Francisco cable car system") carried San Franciscans up [Clay Street](/wiki/Clay_Street_Hill_Railroad "Clay Street Hill Railroad") in 1873. The city's sea of [Victorian houses](/wiki/Victorian_house "Victorian house") began to take shape, and civic leaders campaigned for a spacious public park, resulting in plans for [Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park"). San Franciscans built schools, churches, theaters, and all the hallmarks of civic life. The [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") developed into the most important American military installation on the Pacific coast.[[82]](#cite_note-85)

In 1890, San Francisco's population approached 300,000, making it the [eighth-largest city](/wiki/Largest_cities_in_the_United_States_by_population_by_decade#1890 "Largest cities in the United States by population by decade") in the United States at the time. Around 1901, San Francisco was a major city known for its flamboyant style, stately hotels, ostentatious mansions on [Nob Hill](/wiki/Nob_Hill,_San_Francisco "Nob Hill, San Francisco"), and a thriving arts scene.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200044–55-86)In addition to the population boom, the first North American plague epidemic was the [San Francisco plague of 1900–1904](/wiki/San_Francisco_plague_of_1900%E2%80%931904 "San Francisco plague of 1900–1904").

### Modern American era (1906–1940)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern American era (1906–1940)")]

See also: [ATCOR § San Francisco (1907-1930)](/wiki/ATCOR#San_Francisco_(1907-1930) "ATCOR")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Post-and-Grant-Avenue-Look.jpg/250px-Post-and-Grant-Avenue-Look.jpg)](/wiki/File:Post-and-Grant-Avenue-Look.jpg)

The [1906 San Francisco earthquake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake") was the [deadliest earthquake in U.S. history](/wiki/List_of_disasters_in_the_United_States_by_death_toll "List of disasters in the United States by death toll").

At 5:12 am on April 18, 1906, a major [earthquake struck San Francisco](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake") and northern California. As buildings collapsed from the quake, ruptured [gas lines](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport") ignited fires that spread across the city and burned out of control for several days. With [water mains](/wiki/Water_main "Water main") out of service, the [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") Artillery Corps attempted to contain the inferno by dynamiting entire city blocks to create firebreaks.[[84]](#cite_note-Funston-87) After the flames died down, it was estimated that more than three-quarters of the city lay in ruins, including the entire downtown core.[[27]](#cite_note-Montagne-29) Contemporary accounts reported that 498 people died, though modern estimates put the number far higher in the several thousands.[[85]](#cite_note-88) More than half of the city's population of 400,000 was left homeless.[[86]](#cite_note-89) [Refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") settled temporarily in makeshift tent villages throughout the city, including in Golden Gate Park, the Presidio, and on the beaches. [Jack London](/wiki/Jack_London "Jack London") is remembered for having famously eulogized the earthquake: "Not in history has a modern imperial city been so completely destroyed. San Francisco is gone."[[87]](#cite_note-90)

Rebuilding was rapid and performed on a grand scale. Rejecting calls to completely remake the street grid, San Franciscans opted for speed.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200056–62-91) [Amadeo Giannini](/wiki/Amadeo_Giannini "Amadeo Giannini")'s [Bank of Italy](/wiki/Bank_of_Italy_(United_States) "Bank of Italy (United States)"), later to become [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), provided loans for many of those whose livelihoods had been devastated. The influential [San Francisco Planning and Urban Research Association](/wiki/San_Francisco_Planning_and_Urban_Research_Association "San Francisco Planning and Urban Research Association") or SPUR was founded in 1910 to address the quality of housing after the earthquake.[[89]](#cite_note-92) The earthquake hastened development of western neighborhoods that survived the fire, including [Pacific Heights](/wiki/Pacific_Heights,_San_Francisco "Pacific Heights, San Francisco"), where many of the city's wealthy rebuilt their homes.[[90]](#cite_note-93) In turn, the destroyed mansions of Nob Hill became grand hotels. [City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall "San Francisco City Hall") rose again in the [Beaux Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") style, and the city celebrated its rebirth at the [Panama–Pacific International Exposition](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93Pacific_International_Exposition "Panama–Pacific International Exposition") in 1915.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley20009-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_Panama-Pacific_International_Exposition%2C_San_Francisco%2C_aeroplane_view%2C_1915.jpg/500px-Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_Panama-Pacific_International_Exposition%2C_San_Francisco%2C_aeroplane_view%2C_1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_Panama-Pacific_International_Exposition,_San_Francisco,_aeroplane_view,_1915.jpg)

The [Panama–Pacific Exposition](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93Pacific_International_Exposition_(1915) "Panama–Pacific International Exposition (1915)"), a major [world's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") held in 1915, was seen as a chance to showcase the city's recovery from the earthquake.

During this period, San Francisco built some of its most important infrastructure. Civil Engineer [Michael O'Shaughnessy](/wiki/Michael_O%27Shaughnessy "Michael O'Shaughnessy") was hired by San Francisco Mayor [James Rolph](/wiki/James_Rolph "James Rolph") as chief engineer for the city in September 1912 to supervise the construction of the Twin Peaks Reservoir, the [Stockton Street Tunnel](/wiki/Stockton_Street_Tunnel "Stockton Street Tunnel"), the [Twin Peaks Tunnel](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_Tunnel "Twin Peaks Tunnel"), the [San Francisco Municipal Railway](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Railway "San Francisco Municipal Railway"), the [Auxiliary Water Supply System](/wiki/San_Francisco_Fire_Department_Auxiliary_Water_Supply_System "San Francisco Fire Department Auxiliary Water Supply System"), and new sewers. San Francisco's streetcar system was pushed to completion by O'Shaughnessy between 1915 and 1927. It was the [O'Shaughnessy Dam](/wiki/Hetch_Hetchy "Hetch Hetchy"), [Hetch Hetchy Reservoir](/wiki/Hetch_Hetchy_Reservoir "Hetch Hetchy Reservoir"), and [Hetch Hetchy Aqueduct](/wiki/Hetch_Hetchy_Aqueduct "Hetch Hetchy Aqueduct") that would have the largest effect on San Francisco.[[92]](#cite_note-95) An abundant water supply enabled San Francisco to develop into the city it has become today.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/SF-Oakland-Bay-Bridge-Construction.jpg/250px-SF-Oakland-Bay-Bridge-Construction.jpg)](/wiki/File:SF-Oakland-Bay-Bridge-Construction.jpg)

The [Bay Bridge](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") under construction on [Yerba Buena Island](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Island "Yerba Buena Island") in 1935

In ensuing years, the city solidified its standing as a financial capital; in the wake of the [1929 stock market crash](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_of_1929 "Wall Street Crash of 1929"), not a single San Francisco-based bank failed.[[93]](#cite_note-96) It was at the height of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") that San Francisco undertook two great civil engineering projects, simultaneously constructing the [San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") and the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge"), completing them in 1936 and 1937, respectively. It was in this period that the island of [Alcatraz](/wiki/Alcatraz_Island "Alcatraz Island"), a former military stockade, began its service as a federal maximum security prison, housing notorious inmates such as [Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone "Al Capone") and [Robert Franklin Stroud](/wiki/Robert_Franklin_Stroud "Robert Franklin Stroud"), the Birdman of Alcatraz. San Francisco later celebrated its regained grandeur with a [World's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair"), the [Golden Gate International Exposition](/wiki/Golden_Gate_International_Exposition "Golden Gate International Exposition") in 1939–40, creating [Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island,_San_Francisco "Treasure Island, San Francisco") in the middle of the bay to house it.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

### Postmodern American era (1941–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Postmodern American era (1941–present)")]

See also: [San Francisco in the 1970s](/wiki/San_Francisco_in_the_1970s "San Francisco in the 1970s")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Alexander_Loudon%2C_de_Nederlandse_ambassadeur_in_de_Verenigde_Staten%2C_ondertekent_in_aanwezigheid_van_Charles_Olke_%28II%29_van_der_Plas_en_andere_leden_van_de_Nederlandse_delegatie_het_handvast_van_de_Uni%2C_KITLV_403000.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-thumbnail.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexander_Loudon,_de_Nederlandse_ambassadeur_in_de_Verenigde_Staten,_ondertekent_in_aanwezigheid_van_Charles_Olke_(II)_van_der_Plas_en_andere_leden_van_de_Nederlandse_delegatie_het_handvast_van_de_Uni,_KITLV_403000.tiff)

The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") was created in San Francisco in 1945, when the [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") was signed at the [San Francisco Conference](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization").

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [Hunters Point Naval Shipyard](/wiki/San_Francisco_Naval_Shipyard "San Francisco Naval Shipyard") became a hub of activity, and [Fort Mason](/wiki/Fort_Mason "Fort Mason") became the primary port of embarkation for service members shipping out to the [Pacific Theater of Operations](/wiki/Asiatic-Pacific_Theater "Asiatic-Pacific Theater").[[28]](#cite_note-WWII-30) the city-owned [Sharp Park](/wiki/Sharp_Park_Detention_Station "Sharp Park Detention Station") in [Pacifica](/wiki/Pacifica,_California "Pacifica, California") was also used as an [internment camp](/wiki/Japanese_American_internment "Japanese American internment") to detain [Japanese Americans](/wiki/Japanese_Americans "Japanese Americans").**[[95]](#cite_note-dark_past-98)** The explosion of jobs drew many people, especially [African Americans from the South](/wiki/Second_Great_Migration_(African_American) "Second Great Migration (African American)"), to the area. After the end of the war, many military personnel returning from service abroad and civilians who had originally come to work decided to stay. The [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") creating the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") was drafted and signed in San Francisco in 1945 and, in 1951, the [Treaty of San Francisco](/wiki/Treaty_of_San_Francisco "Treaty of San Francisco") re-established peaceful relations between Japan and the [Allied Powers](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II").[[96]](#cite_note-99)

Significant urban planning projects in the 1950s and 1960s involved widespread destruction and redevelopment of west-side neighborhoods and the construction of new [freeways](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway"), of which only a series of short segments were built before being halted by [citizen-led opposition](/wiki/Highway_revolts_in_the_United_States#San_Francisco "Highway revolts in the United States").[[97]](#cite_note-100) The onset of [containerization](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization") made San Francisco's small piers obsolete, and cargo activity moved to the larger [Port of Oakland](/wiki/Port_of_Oakland "Port of Oakland").[[98]](#cite_note-101) The city began to lose industrial jobs and turned to tourism as the most important segment of its economy.[[99]](#cite_note-102) The suburbs experienced rapid growth, and San Francisco underwent significant demographic change, as large segments of the white population left the city, supplanted by an increasing wave of [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") from Asia and Latin America.[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) From 1950 to 1980, the city lost over 10 percent of its population.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/KFRC_Fantasy_Fair_Dryden_Balin_Kantner.png/250px-KFRC_Fantasy_Fair_Dryden_Balin_Kantner.png)](/wiki/File:KFRC_Fantasy_Fair_Dryden_Balin_Kantner.png)

The [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love") in 1967 was an influential [counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") phenomenon with as many as 100,000 people converging in San Francisco's [Haight-Ashbury](/wiki/Haight-Ashbury "Haight-Ashbury") neighborhood.

Over this period, San Francisco became a magnet for America's [counterculture movement](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s"). [Beat Generation](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation") writers fueled the [San Francisco Renaissance](/wiki/San_Francisco_Renaissance "San Francisco Renaissance") and centered on the [North Beach](/wiki/North_Beach,_San_Francisco "North Beach, San Francisco") neighborhood in the 1950s.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley2000240–242-105) [Hippies](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") flocked to [Haight-Ashbury](/wiki/Haight-Ashbury "Haight-Ashbury") in the 1960s, reaching a peak with the 1967 [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love").[[103]](#cite_note-106) In 1974, the [Zebra murders](/wiki/Zebra_murders "Zebra murders") left at least 16 people dead.[[104]](#cite_note-107) In the 1970s, the city became a center of the [gay rights movement](/wiki/LGBTQ_movements "LGBTQ movements"), with the emergence of [The Castro](/wiki/The_Castro,_San_Francisco "The Castro, San Francisco") as an urban [gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village"), the election of [Harvey Milk](/wiki/Harvey_Milk "Harvey Milk") to the [Board of Supervisors](/wiki/San_Francisco_Board_of_Supervisors "San Francisco Board of Supervisors"), and his [assassination](/wiki/Moscone%E2%80%93Milk_assassinations "Moscone–Milk assassinations"), along with that of Mayor [George Moscone](/wiki/George_Moscone "George Moscone"), in 1978.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

[Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), now based in [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"), was founded in San Francisco; the bank completed [555 California Street](/wiki/555_California_Street "555 California Street") in 1969. The [Transamerica Pyramid](/wiki/Transamerica_Pyramid "Transamerica Pyramid") was completed in 1972,[[106]](#cite_note-109) igniting a wave of "[Manhattanization](/wiki/Manhattanization "Manhattanization")" that lasted until the late 1980s, a period of extensive high-rise development downtown.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200095–96-110) The 1980s also saw a dramatic increase in the number of homeless people in the city, an issue that remains today, despite many attempts to address it.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/SF_Transamerica_full_CA.jpg/250px-SF_Transamerica_full_CA.jpg)](/wiki/File:SF_Transamerica_full_CA.jpg)

The [Transamerica Pyramid](/wiki/Transamerica_Pyramid "Transamerica Pyramid"), built in 1972, characterized the [Manhattanization](/wiki/Manhattanization "Manhattanization") of the city's skyline in the 1970–80s.

The [1989 Loma Prieta earthquake](/wiki/1989_Loma_Prieta_earthquake "1989 Loma Prieta earthquake") caused destruction and loss of life throughout the Bay Area. In San Francisco, the quake severely damaged structures in the [Marina](/wiki/Marina_District,_San_Francisco "Marina District, San Francisco") and [South of Market](/wiki/South_of_Market,_San_Francisco "South of Market, San Francisco") districts and precipitated the demolition of the damaged [Embarcadero Freeway](/wiki/California_State_Route_480 "California State Route 480") and much of the damaged [Central Freeway](/wiki/Central_Freeway "Central Freeway"). The reconstruction allowed the city to reclaim [The Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)") as its historic downtown waterfront and revitalizing the [Hayes Valley](/wiki/Hayes_Valley,_San_Francisco "Hayes Valley, San Francisco") neighborhood.[[109]](#cite_note-112)

The three recent decades have seen booms driven by the internet industry. During the [dot-com boom](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble") of the late 1990s, [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") invigorated the San Francisco economy. Large numbers of entrepreneurs and computer application developers moved into the city, followed by marketing, design, and sales professionals, changing the social landscape as once poorer neighborhoods became increasingly [gentrified](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification").[[110]](#cite_note-113) Demand for new housing and office space ignited a second wave of high-rise development, this time in the South of Market district.[[111]](#cite_note-114) By 2000, the city's population reached new highs, surpassing the previous record set in 1950. When the bubble burst in 2001, many of these companies folded and their employees were laid off. Yet high technology and entrepreneurship remain mainstays of the San Francisco economy. By the mid-2000s (decade), the [social media boom](/wiki/Web_2.0 "Web 2.0") had begun, with San Francisco becoming a popular location for tech offices and a common place to live for people employed in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") companies such as [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") and [Google](/wiki/Google "Google").[[c]](#cite_note-116)

The early 2020s saw a reduction of tech companies' presence in Downtown San Francisco in the wake of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), increased popularity of working at home, and struggles with homelessness. Although some observers have raised the possibility that office vacancies and declining tax revenues could cause San Francisco to enter an economic [doom loop](/wiki/Urban_doom_loop "Urban doom loop"),[[113]](#cite_note-SanFranDoomLoop-117)[[114]](#cite_note-SanFranDoomLoopCurbed-118) other sources have refuted this broad-based characterization of the city as a whole, asserting that the issues of concern are restricted primarily to the urban core of San Francisco.[[115]](#cite_note-DowntownSanFranDying-119)[[116]](#cite_note-SanFranDoomLoopRefute-120)

The [San Francisco Armory](/wiki/San_Francisco_Armory "San Francisco Armory"), [Palace of Fine Arts](/wiki/Palace_of_Fine_Arts "Palace of Fine Arts"), [Haas–Lilienthal House](/wiki/Haas%E2%80%93Lilienthal_House "Haas–Lilienthal House"), and [450 Sutter Street](/wiki/450_Sutter_Street "450 Sutter Street") are among the dozens of historical landmarks in the city [listed on the National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_San_Francisco "National Register of Historic Places listings in San Francisco").[[117]](#cite_note-121)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![San Francisco cityscape and East Bay, from Pikes Peak, above the Castro](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/San_Francisco_cityscape_and_East_Bay%2C_from_Twin_Peaks%2C_above_the_Castro.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_cityscape_and_East_Bay%2C_from_Twin_Peaks%2C_above_the_Castro.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_cityscape_and_East_Bay,_from_Twin_Peaks,_above_the_Castro.jpg)

The San Francisco cityscape and [East Bay](/wiki/East_Bay "East Bay"), seen from [Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_(San_Francisco) "Twin Peaks (San Francisco)"), above the [Castro](/wiki/Castro_District,_San_Francisco "Castro District, San Francisco").

San Francisco is located on the [West Coast of the United States](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"), at the north end of the [San Francisco Peninsula](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peninsula "San Francisco Peninsula") and includes significant stretches of the Pacific Ocean and [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay") within its boundaries. Several picturesque [islands](/wiki/Islands_of_San_Francisco_Bay "Islands of San Francisco Bay")—[Alcatraz](/wiki/Alcatraz_Island "Alcatraz Island"), [Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island,_San_Francisco "Treasure Island, San Francisco") and the adjacent [Yerba Buena Island](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Island "Yerba Buena Island"), and small portions of [Alameda Island](/wiki/Alameda_(island) "Alameda (island)"), [Red Rock Island](/wiki/Red_Rock_Island "Red Rock Island"), and [Angel Island](/wiki/Angel_Island,_California "Angel Island, California")—are part of the city. Also included are the uninhabited [Farallon Islands](/wiki/Farallon_Islands "Farallon Islands"), 27 miles (43 km) offshore in the Pacific Ocean. The mainland within the city limits roughly forms a "seven-by-seven-mile square", a common local colloquialism referring to the city's shape, though its total area, including water, is nearly 232 square miles (600 km2).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/San_Francisco_Peninsula_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2019-03-11_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Peninsula_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2019-03-11_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Peninsula_by_Sentinel-2,_2019-03-11_(cropped).jpg)

A satellite view of San Francisco

There are more than 50 hills within the city limits.[[118]](#cite_note-peak_experience-122) Some neighborhoods are named after the hill on which they are situated, including [Nob Hill](/wiki/Nob_Hill,_San_Francisco "Nob Hill, San Francisco"), [Potrero Hill](/wiki/Potrero_Hill "Potrero Hill"), and [Russian Hill](/wiki/Russian_Hill,_San_Francisco "Russian Hill, San Francisco"). Near the geographic center of the city, southwest of the downtown area, are a series of less densely populated hills. [Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_(San_Francisco) "Twin Peaks (San Francisco)"), a pair of hills forming one of the city's highest points, forms an overlook spot. San Francisco's tallest hill, [Mount Davidson](/wiki/Mount_Davidson_(California) "Mount Davidson (California)"), is 928 feet (283 m) high and is capped with a 103-foot (31 m) tall cross built in 1934.[[119]](#cite_note-123) Dominating this area is [Sutro Tower](/wiki/Sutro_Tower "Sutro Tower"), a large red and white radio and television transmission tower reaching 1,811 ft (552 m) above sea level.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Lake_Merced_Park%2C_Fort_Funston%2C_and_SF_Zoo_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lake_Merced_Park%2C_Fort_Funston%2C_and_SF_Zoo_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Merced_Park,_Fort_Funston,_and_SF_Zoo_(cropped).jpg)

[Lake Merced](/wiki/Lake_Merced "Lake Merced"), located in southwestern San Francisco

The nearby [San Andreas](/wiki/San_Andreas_Fault "San Andreas Fault") and [Hayward Faults](/wiki/Hayward_Fault "Hayward Fault") are responsible for much earthquake activity, although neither physically passes through the city itself. The San Andreas Fault caused the earthquakes in 1906 and 1989. Minor earthquakes occur on a regular basis. The threat of major earthquakes plays a large role in the city's infrastructure development. The city constructed an [auxiliary water supply system](/wiki/San_Francisco_Fire_Department_Auxiliary_Water_Supply_System "San Francisco Fire Department Auxiliary Water Supply System") and has repeatedly upgraded its building codes, requiring retrofits for older buildings and higher engineering standards for new construction.[[120]](#cite_note-124) However, there are still thousands of smaller buildings that remain vulnerable to quake damage.[[121]](#cite_note-125) USGS has released the [California](/wiki/California "California") earthquake forecast which models earthquake occurrence in California.[[122]](#cite_note-126)

San Francisco's shoreline has grown beyond its natural limits. Entire neighborhoods such as the [Marina](/wiki/Marina_District,_San_Francisco "Marina District, San Francisco"), [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Francisco "Mission Bay, San Francisco"), and [Hunters Point](/wiki/Hunters_Point,_San_Francisco "Hunters Point, San Francisco"), as well as large sections of the [Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)"), sit on areas of [landfill](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation"). Treasure Island was constructed from material dredged from the bay as well as material resulting from the excavation of the [Yerba Buena Tunnel](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Tunnel "Yerba Buena Tunnel") through [Yerba Buena Island](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Island "Yerba Buena Island") during the construction of the Bay Bridge. Such land tends to be unstable during earthquakes. The resulting [soil liquefaction](/wiki/Soil_liquefaction "Soil liquefaction") causes extensive damage to property built upon it, as was evidenced in the Marina district during the [1989 Loma Prieta earthquake](/wiki/1989_Loma_Prieta_earthquake "1989 Loma Prieta earthquake").[[123]](#cite_note-127)

A few natural lakes and creeks ([Lake Merced](/wiki/Lake_Merced "Lake Merced"), [Mountain Lake](/wiki/Mountain_Lake_Park "Mountain Lake Park"), [Pine Lake](/wiki/Pine_Lake_(San_Francisco) "Pine Lake (San Francisco)"), [Lobos Creek](/wiki/Lobos_Creek "Lobos Creek"), [El Polin Spring](/wiki/El_Polin_Spring "El Polin Spring")) are within parks and remain protected in what is essentially their original form, but most of the city's natural watercourses, such as [Islais Creek](/wiki/Islais_Creek "Islais Creek") and [Mission Creek](/wiki/Mission_Creek "Mission Creek"), have been partially or completely [culverted](/wiki/Culvert "Culvert") and built over. Since the 1990s, the [Public Utilities Commission](/wiki/San_Francisco_Public_Utilities_Commission "San Francisco Public Utilities Commission") has been studying proposals to daylight or restore some creeks.[[124]](#cite_note-128)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Main articles: [Neighborhoods in San Francisco](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_San_Francisco "Neighborhoods in San Francisco") and [List of Landmarks and Historic Places in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_Landmarks_and_Historic_Places_in_San_Francisco "List of Landmarks and Historic Places in San Francisco")

See also: [List of tallest buildings in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_San_Francisco "List of tallest buildings in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/San_Francisco_in_2018.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_in_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_in_2018.jpg)

A view of San Francisco's central districts along its northeastern coastline

An *Historical Center of San Francisco* monument,[[125]](#cite_note-sfG/rs/unP-129) where the [1899–1906 City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall#History "San Francisco City Hall") was once located,[[126]](#cite_note-a&A/Csf-130) is in [United Nations Plaza](/wiki/United_Nations_Plaza_(San_Francisco) "United Nations Plaza (San Francisco)"), at [37°46′48″N 122°24′49″W﻿ / ﻿37.7800397°N 122.4135943°W﻿ / 37.7800397; -122.4135943](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=San_Francisco&params=37.7800397_N_122.4135943_W_)

The earliest center of San Francisco is [Portsmouth Square](/wiki/Portsmouth_Square "Portsmouth Square"), in the northeast quadrant of the city anchored by [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(San_Francisco) "Market Street (San Francisco)") and the waterfront. Here in the northeast quadrant, the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco") is centered, with [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square_(San_Francisco) "Union Square (San Francisco)"), the principal shopping and hotel district, and the [Tenderloin](/wiki/Tenderloin,_San_Francisco "Tenderloin, San Francisco") nearby. [Cable cars](/wiki/Cable_car_(railway) "Cable car (railway)") carry riders up steep inclines to the summit of [Nob Hill](/wiki/Nob_Hill "Nob Hill"), once the home of the city's business tycoons, and down to the waterfront tourist attractions of [Fisherman's Wharf](/wiki/Fisherman%27s_Wharf,_San_Francisco "Fisherman's Wharf, San Francisco"), and [Pier 39](/wiki/Pier_39 "Pier 39"), where many restaurants feature [Dungeness crab](/wiki/Dungeness_crab "Dungeness crab") from a still-active fishing industry. Also in this quadrant are [Russian Hill](/wiki/Russian_Hill,_San_Francisco "Russian Hill, San Francisco"), a residential neighborhood with the famously crooked [Lombard Street](/wiki/Lombard_Street_(San_Francisco) "Lombard Street (San Francisco)"); [North Beach](/wiki/North_Beach,_San_Francisco "North Beach, San Francisco"), the city's [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy "Little Italy") and the former center of the [Beat Generation](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation"); and [Telegraph Hill](/wiki/Telegraph_Hill,_San_Francisco "Telegraph Hill, San Francisco"), which features [Coit Tower](/wiki/Coit_Tower "Coit Tower"). Abutting Russian Hill and North Beach is San Francisco's [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco"), the oldest [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown") in North America.[[127]](#cite_note-131)[[128]](#cite_note-132)[[129]](#cite_note-Bacon,_Daniel_pages_52-53-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134)

The [South of Market](/wiki/South_of_Market,_San_Francisco "South of Market, San Francisco"), which was once San Francisco's industrial core, has seen significant redevelopment following the construction of [Oracle Park](/wiki/Oracle_Park "Oracle Park") and an infusion of [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"). New skyscrapers, live-work lofts, and condominiums dot the area. Further development is taking place just to the south in [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Francisco "Mission Bay, San Francisco") area, a former railroad yard, which now has a second campus of the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") and [Chase Center](/wiki/Chase_Center "Chase Center"), which opened in [2019](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Golden_State_Warriors_season "2019–20 Golden State Warriors season") as the new home of the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors").[[131]](#cite_note-135)

West of downtown, across [Van Ness Avenue](/wiki/Van_Ness_Avenue_(San_Francisco) "Van Ness Avenue (San Francisco)"), lies the large [Western Addition](/wiki/Western_Addition,_San_Francisco "Western Addition, San Francisco") neighborhood, which became established with a large African American population after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The Western Addition is usually divided into smaller neighborhoods including [Hayes Valley](/wiki/Hayes_Valley,_San_Francisco "Hayes Valley, San Francisco"), [the Fillmore](/wiki/Fillmore_District,_San_Francisco "Fillmore District, San Francisco"), and [Japantown](/wiki/Japantown,_San_Francisco "Japantown, San Francisco"), which was once the largest Japantown in North America but suffered when its [Japanese American](/wiki/Japanese_American "Japanese American") residents were [forcibly removed and interned](/wiki/Japanese_American_internment "Japanese American internment") during World War II. The Western Addition survived the [1906 earthquake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake") with its [Victorians](/wiki/Victorian_house "Victorian house") largely intact, including the famous "[Painted Ladies](/wiki/Painted_Ladies "Painted Ladies")", standing alongside [Alamo Square](/wiki/Alamo_Square "Alamo Square"). To the south, near the geographic center of the city is [Haight-Ashbury](/wiki/Haight-Ashbury "Haight-Ashbury"), famously associated with 1960s [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") culture.[[132]](#cite_note-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/SF_Chinatown_CA_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-SF_Chinatown_CA_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SF_Chinatown_CA_(cropped).jpg)

[San Francisco Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco"), the oldest in North America and one of the world's largest.

North of the Western Addition is [Pacific Heights](/wiki/Pacific_Heights,_San_Francisco "Pacific Heights, San Francisco"), an affluent neighborhood that features the homes built by wealthy San Franciscans in the wake of the 1906 earthquake. Directly north of Pacific Heights facing the waterfront is the [Marina](/wiki/Marina_District,_San_Francisco "Marina District, San Francisco"), a neighborhood popular with young professionals that was largely built on reclaimed land from the Bay.[[133]](#cite_note-137)

In the southeast quadrant of the city is the [Mission District](/wiki/Mission_District "Mission District")—populated in the 19th century by [Californios](/wiki/Californios "Californios") and working-class immigrants from Germany, Ireland, Italy, and Scandinavia. In the 1910s, a wave of Central American immigrants settled in the Mission and, in the 1950s, immigrants from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") began to predominate.[[134]](#cite_note-138) In recent years, gentrification has changed the demographics of parts of the Mission from Latino, to [twenty-something](/wiki/Twentysomething_(term) "Twentysomething (term)") professionals. [Noe Valley](/wiki/Noe_Valley,_San_Francisco "Noe Valley, San Francisco") to the southwest and [Bernal Heights](/wiki/Bernal_Heights,_San_Francisco "Bernal Heights, San Francisco") to the south are both increasingly popular among young families with children.

East of the Mission is the [Potrero Hill](/wiki/Potrero_Hill "Potrero Hill") neighborhood, a mostly residential neighborhood that features sweeping views of downtown San Francisco. West of the Mission, the area historically known as [Eureka Valley](/wiki/Eureka_Valley,_San_Francisco "Eureka Valley, San Francisco"), now popularly called [the Castro](/wiki/The_Castro,_San_Francisco "The Castro, San Francisco"), was once a working-class Scandinavian and Irish area. It has become North America's first [gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village"), and is now the center of [gay](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") life in the city.[[135]](#cite_note-SFGateCastro-139)

Located near the city's southern border, the [Excelsior District](/wiki/Excelsior_District,_San_Francisco "Excelsior District, San Francisco") is one of the most ethnically diverse neighborhoods in San Francisco. The [Bayview-Hunters Point](/wiki/Bayview%E2%80%93Hunters_Point,_San_Francisco "Bayview–Hunters Point, San Francisco") in the far southeast corner of the city is one of the poorest neighborhoods, though the area has been the focus of several revitalizing and [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") projects.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/FerryBuildingEmbarcaderoBayBridge.JPG/250px-FerryBuildingEmbarcaderoBayBridge.JPG)](/wiki/File:FerryBuildingEmbarcaderoBayBridge.JPG)

The [Ferry Building](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ferry_Building "San Francisco Ferry Building"), located in the [Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)"), the city's eastern waterfront along San Francisco Bay

The [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(San_Francisco) "West Side (San Francisco)") of the city remained largely undeveloped until the early 20th century, due to the region being isolated from downtown because of the rugged terrain of Twin Peaks, Mount Sutro and Mount Davidson. Vast sand dunes made up the costal plain with the uninhibited area being dubbed the [Outside Lands](/wiki/Outside_Lands "Outside Lands"). The creation of Golden Gate Park in 1870–1900 contributed to the area being developed. The construction of the [Twin Peaks Tunnel](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_Tunnel "Twin Peaks Tunnel") in 1918 connected southwest neighborhoods to downtown via streetcar, hastening the development of [West Portal](/wiki/West_Portal,_San_Francisco "West Portal, San Francisco"), and nearby affluent [Forest Hill](/wiki/Forest_Hill,_San_Francisco "Forest Hill, San Francisco") and [St. Francis Wood](/wiki/St._Francis_Wood,_San_Francisco "St. Francis Wood, San Francisco"). Further west, stretching all the way to the Pacific Ocean and north to [Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park") lies the vast [Sunset District](/wiki/Sunset_District,_San_Francisco "Sunset District, San Francisco"), a large middle-class area with a predominantly Asian population.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

The northwestern quadrant of the city contains the [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond_District,_San_Francisco "Richmond District, San Francisco"), a mostly middle-class neighborhood north of Golden Gate Park, home to immigrants from other parts of Asia as well as many [Russian](/wiki/Russian_people "Russian people") and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_people "Ukrainian people") immigrants. Together, these areas are known as [The Avenues](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_San_Francisco "Neighborhoods in San Francisco").

Many piers remained derelict for years until the demolition of the [Embarcadero Freeway](/wiki/Embarcadero_Freeway "Embarcadero Freeway") reopened the downtown waterfront, allowing for redevelopment. The centerpiece of the port, the [Ferry Building](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ferry_Building "San Francisco Ferry Building"), while still receiving commuter ferry traffic, has been restored and redeveloped as a gourmet marketplace.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/San_francisco_in_fog_with_rays.jpg/250px-San_francisco_in_fog_with_rays.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_francisco_in_fog_with_rays.jpg)

[San Francisco fog](/wiki/San_Francisco_fog "San Francisco fog") is a regular phenomenon in the summer.

San Francisco has a [warm-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Warm-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Warm-summer Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csb*, [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"): *Csll*), characteristic of California's coast, with cool, moist winters and mild dry summers with occasional warm or hot periods.[[137]](#cite_note-141) San Francisco's weather is strongly influenced by the [cool currents](/wiki/California_Current "California Current") of the Pacific Ocean on the west side of the city, and the water of [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay") to the north and east. This moderates temperature swings and produces a remarkably mild year-round climate with little seasonal temperature variation.[[138]](#cite_note-142)

Among major U.S. cities, San Francisco has the coolest daily mean, maximum, and minimum temperatures for June, July, and August.[[139]](#cite_note-143) During the summer, rising hot air in California's interior valleys creates a low-pressure area that draws winds from the [North Pacific High](/wiki/North_Pacific_High "North Pacific High") through the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate"), which creates the city's [characteristic cool winds and fog](/wiki/San_Francisco_fog "San Francisco fog").[[140]](#cite_note-144) The fog is less pronounced in eastern neighborhoods and during the late summer and early fall. The year's warmest month, on average, is September, and on average, October is warmer than July, especially in daytime.

Temperatures reach or exceed 80 °F (27 °C) on an average of 20 to 25 days a year and at downtown and [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport") (SFO), respectively.[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145) The dry period of May to October is mild to warm, with the normal monthly mean temperature peaking in September at 62.7 °F (17.1 °C).[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145) The rainy period of November to April is slightly cooler, with the normal monthly mean temperature reaching its lowest in January at 51.3 °F (10.7 °C).[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145)

On average, there are 73 rainy days a year, and annual precipitation averages 23.65 inches (601 mm).[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145) Variation in precipitation from year to year is high. Above-average rain years are often associated with warm [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") conditions in the Pacific while dry years often occur in cold water [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña") periods. In 2013 (a "La Niña" year), a record low 5.59 in (142 mm) of rainfall was recorded at downtown San Francisco, where records have been kept since 1849.[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145) Snowfall in the city is very rare, with only 10 measurable accumulations recorded since 1852, most recently in 1976 when up to 5 inches (13 cm) fell on Twin Peaks and up to an inch 1 inch (2.5 cm) in downtown San Francisco.[[142]](#cite_note-146)[[143]](#cite_note-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/View_from_Mirounga_Bay.jpg/250px-View_from_Mirounga_Bay.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_Mirounga_Bay.jpg)

The [Farallon Islands](/wiki/Farallon_Islands "Farallon Islands") are located in the [Gulf of the Farallones](/wiki/Gulf_of_the_Farallones "Gulf of the Farallones"), off the [Pacific coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") of San Francisco.

The highest recorded temperature at the official [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service") downtown observation station[[d]](#cite_note-149) was 106 °F (41 °C) on September 1, 2017.[[145]](#cite_note-150) During that hot spell, the warmest ever night of 71 °F (22 °C) was also recorded.[[146]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA2-151) The lowest recorded temperature was 27 °F (−3 °C) on December 11, 1932.[[147]](#cite_note-152)

During an average year between 1991 and 2020, San Francisco recorded a warmest night at 64 °F (18 °C) and a coldest day at 49 °F (9 °C).[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145) The coldest daytime high since the station's opening in 1945 was recorded in December 1972 at 37 °F (3 °C).[[141]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_NOAA-145)

As a coastal city, San Francisco will be heavily affected by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), sea levels are projected to rise by as much as 5 feet (1.5 m), resulting in periodic flooding, rising groundwater levels, and lowland floods from more severe storms.[[148]](#cite_note-153)

San Francisco falls under the [USDA](/wiki/USDA "USDA") Plant [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 10a and 10b.[[149]](#cite_note-154)[[150]](#cite_note-155)
