### Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ecology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Aerial_view_-_Presidio-whole.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_-_Presidio-whole.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_-_Presidio-whole.jpg)

An aerial view of the [Presidio of San Francisco](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") and the [Golden Gate](/wiki/Golden_Gate "Golden Gate")

Historically, [tule elk](/wiki/Tule_elk "Tule elk") were present in San Francisco County, based on archeological evidence of elk remains in at least five different [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") shellmounds: at Hunter's Point, Fort Mason, Stevenson Street, Market Street, and Yerba Buena.[[156]](#cite_note-163)[[157]](#cite_note-164) Perhaps the first historical observer record was from the [De Anza Expedition](/wiki/De_Anza_Expedition "De Anza Expedition") on March 23, 1776. [Herbert Eugene Bolton](/wiki/Herbert_Eugene_Bolton "Herbert Eugene Bolton") wrote about the expedition camp at Mountain Lake, near the southern end of today's [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco"): "Round about were grazing deer, and scattered here and there were the antlers of large elk."[[158]](#cite_note-165)

According to the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), San Francisco's population was 873,965, an increase of 8.5% from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[11]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-11) The city is the [second-most densely populated large American city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density "List of United States cities by population density"), behind only New York City among cities with populations greater than 200,000. The county, which is the same boundaries as the city, is the [fifth-most densely populated U.S. county](/wiki/County_statistics_of_the_United_States "County statistics of the United States"), following four of the five New York City [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City").

San Francisco is part of the five-county [San Francisco–Oakland–Hayward, CA Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland%E2%80%93Hayward,_CA_Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "San Francisco–Oakland–Hayward, CA Metropolitan Statistical Area"), a region of 4.7 million people ([13th most populous in the U.S.](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area")), and has served as its traditional demographic focal point. It is also part of the greater 14-county [San Jose-San Francisco-Oakland, CA Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/San_Jose-San_Francisco-Oakland,_CA_Combined_Statistical_Area "San Jose-San Francisco-Oakland, CA Combined Statistical Area"), whose population is over 9.6 million, making it the [fifth-largest in the United States](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit).[[159]](#cite_note-166)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Race, ethnicity, religion, and languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Race, ethnicity, religion, and languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Ethnic_Origins_in_San_Francisco.png/500px-Ethnic_Origins_in_San_Francisco.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_San_Francisco.png)

Ethnic origins in San Francisco

As of the 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit) census, the racial makeup and population of San Francisco included: 361,382 [Whites](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") (41.3%), 296,505 [Asians](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") (33.9%), 46,725 [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans_in_San_Francisco "African Americans in San Francisco") (5.3%), 86,233 [Multiracial Americans](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans") (9.9%), 6,475 [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") (0.7%), 3,476 [Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") and other [Pacific Islanders](/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Americans "Pacific Islands Americans") (0.4%) and 73,169 persons of other races (8.4%). There were 136,761 [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans_in_San_Francisco "Hispanic and Latino Americans in San Francisco") residents of any race (15.6%).

San Francisco is a [majority minority](/wiki/Majority_minority "Majority minority") city, as [non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White") residents comprise less than half of the population; in 1940 they formed 92.5% of the population.[[160]](#cite_note-census-167)

In 2010, residents of [Chinese ethnicity](/wiki/Chinese_Americans_in_San_Francisco "Chinese Americans in San Francisco") constituted the largest single ethnic minority group in San Francisco at 21% of the population; other large Asian groups include [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipino_Americans "Filipino Americans") (5%) and [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_Americans "Vietnamese Americans") (2%), with [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_in_San_Francisco "Japanese in San Francisco"), [Koreans](/wiki/Korean_Americans "Korean Americans") and many other Asian and Pacific Islander groups represented in the city.[[161]](#cite_note-Census_2010-GCT-PH1-racial-168) The population of Chinese ancestry is most heavily concentrated in Chinatown and the [Sunset](/wiki/Sunset_District,_San_Francisco "Sunset District, San Francisco") and [Richmond Districts](/wiki/Richmond_District,_San_Francisco "Richmond District, San Francisco"). [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos "Filipinos") are most concentrated in [SoMa](/wiki/South_of_Market,_San_Francisco "South of Market, San Francisco") and the [Crocker-Amazon](/wiki/Crocker-Amazon "Crocker-Amazon"); the latter neighborhood shares a border with [Daly City](/wiki/Daly_City,_California "Daly City, California"), which has one of the highest concentrations of Filipinos in North America.[[161]](#cite_note-Census_2010-GCT-PH1-racial-168)[[162]](#cite_note-169) The [Tenderloin District](/wiki/Tenderloin,_San_Francisco "Tenderloin, San Francisco") is home to a large portion of the city's Vietnamese population as well as businesses and restaurants, which is known as the city's Little Saigon.[[161]](#cite_note-Census_2010-GCT-PH1-racial-168)

The principal [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans_in_San_Francisco "Hispanic and Latino Americans in San Francisco") groups in the city were those of [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") (7%) and [Salvadoran](/wiki/Salvadoran_Americans "Salvadoran Americans") (2%) ancestry. The Hispanic population is most heavily concentrated in the [Mission District](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco"), Tenderloin District, and [Excelsior District](/wiki/Excelsior_District,_San_Francisco "Excelsior District, San Francisco").[[163]](#cite_note-interactivemap-170) The city's percentage of Hispanic residents is less than half of that of the state.

[African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans_in_San_Francisco "African Americans in San Francisco") constituted about 5% of San Francisco's population in 2020; their share of the city's population has been decreasing since the 1970s.[[164]](#cite_note-171) The majority of [the city's Black residents](/wiki/African_Americans_in_San_Francisco "African Americans in San Francisco") live in the neighborhoods of [Bayview-Hunters Point](/wiki/Bayview-Hunters_Point "Bayview-Hunters Point"), [Visitacion Valley](/wiki/Visitacion_Valley "Visitacion Valley"), and the [Fillmore District](/wiki/Fillmore_District "Fillmore District").[[163]](#cite_note-interactivemap-170) There are smaller Black communities in [Diamond Heights](/wiki/Diamond_Heights,_San_Francisco "Diamond Heights, San Francisco"), [Glen Park](/wiki/Glen_Park,_San_Francisco "Glen Park, San Francisco"), and the [Mission District](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco").

Unlike many other Western cities, San Francisco has a significant [Irish American](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") population. Drawn by the California Gold Rush, the Irish were one of the first major White immigrant groups to arrive in California, settling in the city of San Francisco.[[165]](#cite_note-172) The Irish historically settled in [Irish Hill](/wiki/Irish_Hill_(San_Francisco) "Irish Hill (San Francisco)"), which was razed following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[166]](#cite_note-173) Despite this, San Francisco is still one of only three counties in the entire [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States") whose White population is plurality (20.6%) Irish.[[167]](#cite_note-174)[[168]](#cite_note-175)

The city has long been home to a significant Jewish community; in 2018 [Jewish Americans](/wiki/Jewish_Americans "Jewish Americans") made up an estimated 10% (80,000) of the city's population. It the third-largest Jewish community in proportional terms in the United States, behind only those of New York City, and Los Angeles, respectively, and it is also relatively young compared to other major U.S. cities.[[169]](#cite_note-ToI-176) The Jewish community resides throughout the city, but the [Richmond District](/wiki/Richmond_District,_San_Francisco "Richmond District, San Francisco") is home to an ethnic enclave of mostly [Russian Jews](/wiki/Russian_Jews "Russian Jews").[[170]](#cite_note-SF_Travel-177) The [Fillmore District](/wiki/Fillmore_District "Fillmore District") was formerly a mostly Jewish neighborhood from the 1920s until the 1970s, when many of its Jewish residents moved to other neighborhoods of the city as well as the suburbs of nearby [Marin County](/wiki/Marin_County "Marin County").[[171]](#cite_note-Found_SF-178)

A racial distribution map of San Francisco, 2010 U.S. Census. Each dot is 25 people: ⬤ White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Other

In a 2018 study by the Jewish Community Federation of San Francisco, Jews make up 10% (80,000) of the city's population, making Judaism the second-largest religion in San Francisco after Christianity.[[169]](#cite_note-ToI-176) In a 2014 study by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), the largest religious groupings in San Francisco's [metropolitan area](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland%E2%80%93Hayward,_CA_Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "San Francisco–Oakland–Hayward, CA Metropolitan Statistical Area") are Christians (48%), followed by those of [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (35%), Hindus (5%), Jews (3%), Buddhists (2%), Muslims (1%) and a variety of other religions have smaller followings. In 2014, about 20% of residents in the area are [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), and 25% professing [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") beliefs. 10% of the residents in metropolitan San Francisco identify as [agnostics](/wiki/Agnostics "Agnostics"), while 5% identify as [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism").[[173]](#cite_note-181)[[174]](#cite_note-182)

In 2010, 55% (411,728) of San Francisco residents spoke only English at home, while 19% (140,302) spoke a [variety of Chinese](/wiki/Variety_of_Chinese "Variety of Chinese") (mostly [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese") and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")[[175]](#cite_note-183)[[176]](#cite_note-184)), 12% (88,147) Spanish, 3% (25,767) [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), and 2% (14,017) Russian. In total, 45% (342,693) of San Francisco's population spoke a language at home other than English.[[177]](#cite_note-185)

#### Ethnic clustering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ethnic clustering")]

San Francisco has several prominent Chinese, Mexican, and Filipino neighborhoods including [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") and [the Mission District](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco"). In 2017, more than half of the Asian population in San Francisco was either Chinese-born (40.3%) or Philippine-born (13.1%). Of the Mexican population, 21% were Mexican-born.[[178]](#cite_note-:02-186) Between 1990 and 2000, the number of foreign-born residents increased from 33% to nearly 40%.[[178]](#cite_note-:02-186) During this same period, the San Francisco metropolitan area received 850,000 immigrants, ranking third in the United States after Los Angeles and New York.[[178]](#cite_note-:02-186)

### Education, households, and income

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education, households, and income")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Seacliff_District_SF.jpg/250px-Seacliff_District_SF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seacliff_District_SF.jpg)

[Sea Cliff](/wiki/Sea_Cliff,_San_Francisco "Sea Cliff, San Francisco") is one of the city's most expensive neighborhoods.[[179]](#cite_note-187)

Of all major cities in the United States, San Francisco has the second-highest percentage of residents with a college degree, second only to [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"). Over 44% of adults have a bachelor's or higher degree.[[180]](#cite_note-Bizjournals-188) San Francisco had the highest rate at 7,031 per square mile, or over 344,000 total graduates in the city's 46.7 square miles (121 km2).[[181]](#cite_note-USA_Today-189)

San Francisco has the highest estimated percentage of gay and lesbian individuals of any of the 50 largest U.S. cities, at 15%.[[182]](#cite_note-190) San Francisco also has the highest percentage of same-sex households of any American county, with the Bay Area having a higher concentration than any other [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area").[[183]](#cite_note-gaystats-191)

San Francisco ranks third of American cities in median household income[[184]](#cite_note-Census_ACS_2003-192) with a 2007 value of $65,519.[[185]](#cite_note-SF_QuickFacts-193) Median family income is $81,136.[[185]](#cite_note-SF_QuickFacts-193) An emigration of middle-class families has left the city with a lower proportion of children than any other large American city,[[186]](#cite_note-194) with the dog population cited as exceeding the child population of 115,000, in 2018.[[187]](#cite_note-195) In 2007, San Francisco's [poverty rate](/wiki/Poverty_rate "Poverty rate") was 12%, lower than the national average.[[188]](#cite_note-196)
[Homelessness has been a chronic problem for San Francisco](/wiki/Homelessness_in_San_Francisco "Homelessness in San Francisco") since the early 1970s.[[189]](#cite_note-197)
The city is believed to have the highest number of homeless inhabitants per capita of any major U.S. city.[[190]](#cite_note-198)[[191]](#cite_note-199)

There are 345,811 households in the city. 133,366 households (39%) were individuals, 109,437 (32%) were [opposite-sex married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage"), 63,577 (18%) had children under the age of 18 living in them, 21,677 (6%) were [unmarried opposite-sex partnerships](/wiki/POSSLQ "POSSLQ"), and 10,384 (3%) were [same-sex married couples or partnerships](/wiki/Same-sex_partnerships "Same-sex partnerships"). The average household size was 2.26. The average family size was 3.11. 452,986 people (56%) lived in rental housing units. 327,985 people (41%) lived in owner-occupied housing units. The median age is 38 years.

San Francisco declared itself a [sanctuary city](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city") in 1989. City officials strengthened the stance in 2013 with its 'Due Process for All' ordinance. The law declared local authorities could not hold immigrants for immigration offenses if they had no violent felonies on their records and did not currently face charges.[[192]](#cite_note-200) The city issues a [Resident ID Card](/wiki/SF_City_ID_Card "SF City ID Card") regardless of the applicant's immigration status.[[193]](#cite_note-201)

### Homelessness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Homelessness")]

See also: [Homelessness in the San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "Homelessness in the San Francisco Bay Area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/San_Francisco_Homeless_Tents_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Homeless_Tents_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Homeless_Tents_(cropped).jpg)

A [homeless encampment](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "Homelessness in the San Francisco Bay Area") under a freeway in San Francisco

[Homelessness in San Francisco](/wiki/Homelessness_in_San_Francisco "Homelessness in San Francisco") emerged as a major issue in the late 20th century and remains a growing problem in modern times.[[194]](#cite_note-202)

In 2019, 8,035 homeless people were counted in San Francisco's point-in-time street and shelter count. This was an increase of more than 17% over the 2017 count of 6,858 people. 5,180 of the people were living unsheltered on the streets and in parks.[[195]](#cite_note-:6-203) 26% of respondents in the 2019 count identified job loss as the primary cause of their homelessness, 18% cited alcohol or drug use, and 13% cited being evicted from their residence.[[195]](#cite_note-:6-203)

The city of San Francisco has been dramatically increasing its spending to service the growing population homelessness crisis: spending jumped by $241 million in 2016–17 to total $275 million, compared to a budget of just $34 million the previous year. In 2017–18 the budget for combatting homelessness stood at $305 million.[[196]](#cite_note-Amy_Graff-204) In the 2019–2020 budget year, the city budgeted $368 million for homelessness services. In the proposed 2020–2021 budget, the city budgeted $850 million for homelessness services.[[197]](#cite_note-205)

In January 2018, a United Nations special rapporteur on homelessness, Leilani Farha, stated that she was "completely shocked" by San Francisco's homelessness crisis during a visit to the city. She compared the "deplorable conditions" of the homeless camps she witnessed on San Francisco's streets to those she had seen in [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai").[[196]](#cite_note-Amy_Graff-204) In May 2020, San Francisco officially sanctioned [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_States "Homelessness in the United States") [encampments](/wiki/List_of_tent_cities_in_the_United_States "List of tent cities in the United States").[[198]](#cite_note-206)

In 2024, according to San Francisco's Point-in-Time (PIT) count, the homeless population was approximately 8,323 individuals. Additionally, over 20,000 people in the city were seeking homeless services.[[199]](#cite_note-207)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [Crime in San Francisco](/wiki/Crime_in_San_Francisco "Crime in San Francisco")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(March 2024)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Police_Horse_with_Giants_Hat_%288146841565%29.jpg/250px-Police_Horse_with_Giants_Hat_%288146841565%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Police_Horse_with_Giants_Hat_(8146841565).jpg)

[SFPD](/wiki/SFPD "SFPD") mounted police officers

San Francisco's violent crime rate is low compared to other major cities.

In 2011, 50 murders were reported, which is 6.1 per 100,000 people.[[200]](#cite_note-208) There were about 134 rapes, 3,142 robberies, and about 2,139 assaults. There were about 4,469 burglaries, 25,100 thefts, and 4,210 motor vehicle thefts.[[201]](#cite_note-209) The [Tenderloin](/wiki/Tenderloin,_San_Francisco "Tenderloin, San Francisco") area has the highest crime rate in San Francisco: 70% of the city's violent crimes, and around one-fourth of the city's murders, occur in this neighborhood. The Tenderloin also sees high rates of drug abuse, gang violence, and prostitution.[[202]](#cite_note-210)

Another area with high crime rates is the [Bayview-Hunters Point](/wiki/Bayview%E2%80%93Hunters_Point,_San_Francisco "Bayview–Hunters Point, San Francisco") area. In the first six months of 2015 there were 25 murders, compared to 14 in the first six months of 2014. However, the murder rate is still much lower than in past decades.[[203]](#cite_note-211) That rate rose again by the close of 2016. According to the San Francisco Police Department, there were 59 murders in the city in 2016, an annual total that marked a 13.5% increase in the number of homicides (52) from 2015.[[204]](#cite_note-212) The city has also gained a reputation for car break-ins, with over 19,000 car break-ins occurring in 2021.[[205]](#cite_note-213)

During the first half of 2018, human feces on San Francisco sidewalks were the second-most-frequent complaint of city residents, with about 65 calls per day. The city has formed a "poop patrol" to attempt to combat the problem.[[206]](#cite_note-sfchroniclepoop-214)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/San_Francisco_Police_Department_vehicles_Market_Street_%28July_2022%29.JPG/250px-San_Francisco_Police_Department_vehicles_Market_Street_%28July_2022%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Police_Department_vehicles_Market_Street_(July_2022).JPG)

SFPD [parking enforcement officers](/wiki/Parking_enforcement_officer "Parking enforcement officer")

Several [street gangs](/wiki/Street_gang "Street gang") have operated in the city over the decades, including [MS-13](/wiki/MS-13 "MS-13"),[[207]](#cite_note-215) the [Sureños](/wiki/Sure%C3%B1os "Sureños") and [Norteños](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1os "Norteños") in the Mission District.[[208]](#cite_note-216)

African-American street gangs familiar in other cities, including the [Bloods](/wiki/Bloods_gang "Bloods gang"), [Crips](/wiki/Crips "Crips"), and their sets, have struggled to establish footholds in San Francisco,[[209]](#cite_note-217) while police and prosecutors have been accused of liberally labeling young African-American males as gang members.[[210]](#cite_note-218)

Criminal [Triad](/wiki/Triad_(organized_crime) "Triad (organized crime)") groups, such as the [Wo Hop To](/wiki/Wo_Hop_To "Wo Hop To"), were active in San Francisco in the 20th century.[[211]](#cite_note-219)

According to statistics released by SFPD in April 2024, the crime figures were down in the first 100 days of the year, namely in terms of robberies, burglaries and larceny.[[212]](#cite_note-220) In the first half of 2024, San Francisco experienced a 32% decrease in overall crime compared to the previous year. Personal property theft saw a 41% reduction, and violent crimes, including gun-related incidents, decreased by 30%.[[213]](#cite_note-221)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [List of companies based in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_San_Francisco "List of companies based in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Montgomery_Street_from_Telegraph_Hill%2C_San_Francisco.jpg/250px-Montgomery_Street_from_Telegraph_Hill%2C_San_Francisco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montgomery_Street_from_Telegraph_Hill,_San_Francisco.jpg)

[San Francisco's Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco") is considered the [Wall Street of the West](/wiki/Wall_Street_West#"Wall_Street_of_the_West" "Wall Street West").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Map_of_Employment_by_City_and_Sector_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area_in_2023.svg/330px-Map_of_Employment_by_City_and_Sector_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area_in_2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Employment_by_City_and_Sector_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area_in_2023.svg)

Map of Jobs in the Bay Area by City and Sector

The city has a diversified [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy"), with employment spread across a wide range of professional services, including [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services"), and [high technology](/wiki/High_tech "High tech").[[214]](#cite_note-caedd-2016-222) In 2016, approximately 27% of workers were employed in professional business services; 14% in leisure and hospitality; 13% in government services; 12% in education and health care; 11% in trade, transportation, and utilities; and 8% in financial activities.[[214]](#cite_note-caedd-2016-222) In 2024, GDP in the nine-county [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area") grew 4.3% in real terms to $1.332 trillion.[[32]](#cite_note-:1-34)

In 2024, the 13-county [San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland](/wiki/San_Jose%E2%80%93San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland,_CA_Combined_Statistical_Area "San Jose–San Francisco–Oakland, CA Combined Statistical Area") [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") had a GDP of $1.408 trillion, ranking 3rd among [CSAs](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area"), and ahead of all but [15 countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), San Francisco County ranked 9th by income per capita among U.S. counties, with a per capita personal income of $171,497.[[14]](#cite_note-2021Pop-15) [Marin County](/wiki/Marin_County,_California "Marin County, California"), directly to the north over the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge"), and [San Mateo County](/wiki/San_Mateo_County,_California "San Mateo County, California"), directly to the south on the [Peninsula](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peninsula "San Francisco Peninsula"), were the 7th and 8th highest-income counties respectively.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/San_Francisco%2C_California_April_2022_skyline_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco%2C_California_April_2022_skyline_%28cropped2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco,_California_April_2022_skyline_(cropped2).jpg)

Skyline of [South of Market](/wiki/South_of_Market,_San_Francisco "South of Market, San Francisco") (SoMa), including [Salesforce Tower](/wiki/Salesforce_Tower "Salesforce Tower"), the [tallest building in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_San_Francisco "List of tallest buildings in San Francisco")

The legacy of the California gold rush turned San Francisco into the principal banking and finance center of the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") in the early twentieth century.[[215]](#cite_note-California2011-223) [Montgomery Street](/wiki/Montgomery_Street "Montgomery Street") in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco") became known as the "[Wall Street of the West](/wiki/Wall_Street_West#San_Francisco "Wall Street West")", home to the [Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_San_Francisco "Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco"), and the site of the now-defunct [Pacific Coast Stock Exchange](/wiki/Pacific_Exchange "Pacific Exchange").[[215]](#cite_note-California2011-223) [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), a pioneer in making banking services accessible to the middle class, was founded in San Francisco and in the 1960s, built the landmark modern skyscraper at [555 California Street](/wiki/555_California_Street "555 California Street") for its corporate headquarters, since relocated to [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina").

Many large financial institutions, multinational banks, and venture capital firms are based in or have regional headquarters in the city. With over 30 international financial institutions,[[216]](#cite_note-CityData.com-224) six [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies,[[217]](#cite_note-225) and a large supporting infrastructure of professional services—including law, public relations, [architecture](/wiki/San_Francisco_architecture "San Francisco architecture") and design—San Francisco is designated as an [Alpha(-) World City](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[218]](#cite_note-226) The 2024 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") ranked San Francisco as the fifth-most competitive financial center in the world.[[219]](#cite_note-:2-227)

Beginning in the 1990s, San Francisco's economy diversified away from finance and tourism towards the growing fields of high tech, [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), and [medical research](/wiki/Medical_research "Medical research").[[220]](#cite_note-Waters2009-228) Technology jobs accounted for just 1 percent of San Francisco's economy in 1990, growing to 4 percent in 2010 and an estimated 8 percent by the end of 2013.[[221]](#cite_note-229) San Francisco became a center of Internet [start-up companies](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") during the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble") of the 1990s and the subsequent [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") boom of the late 2000s (decade).[[222]](#cite_note-230) Since 2010, San Francisco proper has attracted an increasing share of venture capital investments as compared to nearby [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley"), attracting 423 financings worth US$4.58 billion in 2013.[[223]](#cite_note-231)[[224]](#cite_note-232)[[225]](#cite_note-233) In 2004, the city approved a payroll tax exemption for biotechnology companies[[226]](#cite_note-234) to foster growth in the [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Francisco "Mission Bay, San Francisco") neighborhood, site of a second campus and hospital of the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") (UCSF). Mission Bay hosts the [UCSF Medical Center](/wiki/UCSF_Medical_Center "UCSF Medical Center"), the [California Institute for Regenerative Medicine](/wiki/California_Institute_for_Regenerative_Medicine "California Institute for Regenerative Medicine"), [California Institute for Quantitative Biosciences](/wiki/California_Institute_for_Quantitative_Biosciences "California Institute for Quantitative Biosciences"), and [Gladstone Institutes](/wiki/Gladstone_Institutes "Gladstone Institutes"),[[227]](#cite_note-235) as well as more than 40 private-sector life sciences companies.[[228]](#cite_note-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Union_Square_-_San_Francisco_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Union_Square_-_San_Francisco_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Square_-_San_Francisco_(cropped).jpg)

[Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square,_San_Francisco "Union Square, San Francisco") is a major [retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail") hub for San Francisco and the Bay Area.

According to academic Rob Wilson, San Francisco is a [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city"), a status that pre-dated the city's popularity during the [California gold rush](/wiki/California_gold_rush "California gold rush").[[229]](#cite_note-237) However, the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") has led to high office vacancy rates and the accelerating evacuation of many retail and tech businesses out of the downtown core of San Francisco.[[115]](#cite_note-DowntownSanFranDying-119)[[230]](#cite_note-SanFran2025AcceleratingDoomLoop-238)[[231]](#cite_note-SanFranExodus4-239)[[232]](#cite_note-240) Attributed causes include a shift to [remote work](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work") in the technology and professional services sectors, as well as high levels of [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "Homelessness in the San Francisco Bay Area"), drug use, and crime in areas around downtown San Francisco, such as the [Tenderloin](/wiki/Tenderloin,_San_Francisco "Tenderloin, San Francisco") and [Mid-Market](/wiki/Mid-Market,_San_Francisco "Mid-Market, San Francisco") neighborhoods.[[233]](#cite_note-SanFranDoomLoop5-241)[[234]](#cite_note-242)

The top employer in San Francisco is the city government itself, employing 5.6% (35,000+ people) of the city's workforce, followed by [UCSF](/wiki/UCSF "UCSF") with over 29,000 employees.[[235]](#cite_note-243) The largest private-sector employer is [Salesforce](/wiki/Salesforce "Salesforce"), with 11,953 employees, as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit). Small businesses with fewer than 10 employees and self-employed firms made up 85% of city establishments in 2006,[[236]](#cite_note-244) and the number of San Franciscans employed by firms of more than 1,000 employees has fallen by half since 1977.[[237]](#cite_note-ega06b-245) The growth of national [big box](/wiki/Big-box_store "Big-box store") and [formula retail](/wiki/Formula_retail "Formula retail") chains into the city has been made intentionally difficult by political and civic consensus. In an effort to buoy small privately owned businesses in San Francisco and preserve the unique retail personality of the city, the Small Business Commission started a publicity campaign in 2004 to keep a larger share of retail dollars in the local economy,[[238]](#cite_note-246) and the Board of Supervisors has used the planning code to limit the neighborhoods where formula retail establishments can set up shop,[[239]](#cite_note-247) an effort affirmed by San Francisco voters.[[240]](#cite_note-248) However, by 2016, San Francisco was rated low by small businesses in a Business Friendliness Survey.[[241]](#cite_note-249)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/San_Francisco_Ferry_Building_January_2014_002_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Ferry_Building_January_2014_002_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Ferry_Building_January_2014_002_(cropped).jpg)

[Ferry Building](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ferry_Building "San Francisco Ferry Building") in the [Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)").

[Like many U.S. cities](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_the_United_States "Manufacturing in the United States"), San Francisco once had a significant manufacturing sector employing nearly 60,000 workers in 1969, but nearly all production left for cheaper locations by the 1980s.[[242]](#cite_note-Frojo2014-250) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), San Francisco has seen a small resurgence in manufacturing, with more than 4,000 manufacturing jobs across 500 companies, doubling since 2011. The city's largest manufacturing employer is [Anchor Brewing Company](/wiki/Anchor_Brewing_Company "Anchor Brewing Company"), and the largest by revenue is [Timbuk2](/wiki/Timbuk2 "Timbuk2").[[242]](#cite_note-Frojo2014-250)

As of the first quarter of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), the median value of homes in San Francisco County was $1,297,030. It ranked third in the U.S. for counties with highest median home value, behind [Nantucket, Massachusetts](/wiki/Nantucket,_Massachusetts "Nantucket, Massachusetts") and [San Mateo County, California](/wiki/San_Mateo_County,_California "San Mateo County, California").[[243]](#cite_note-251)

### Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Technology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Twitter_Headquarters_in_San_Francisco-L1001299.jpg/250px-Twitter_Headquarters_in_San_Francisco-L1001299.jpg)](/wiki/File:Twitter_Headquarters_in_San_Francisco-L1001299.jpg)

Former [X](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") headquarters on [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(San_Francisco) "Market Street (San Francisco)"), prior to moving to [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas")

San Francisco became a hub for technology-driven economic growth during the [internet boom](/wiki/Internet_boom "Internet boom") of the 1990s, and still holds an important position in the world city network today.[[178]](#cite_note-:02-186)[[244]](#cite_note-:3-252) Intense redevelopment towards the "[new economy](/wiki/New_economy "New economy")" makes business more technologically minded. Between the years of 1999 and 2000, the job growth rate was 4.9%, creating over 50,000 jobs in technology firms and internet content production.[[178]](#cite_note-:02-186) However, the technology industry has become geographically dispersed.[[245]](#cite_note-NewYorkCloserThanEvertoBayAreaTech-253)[[246]](#cite_note-ManhattanNowBiggestTechHub-254)

In the second technological boom driven by social media in the mid-2000s, San Francisco became a location for companies such as [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft_San_Francisco "Ubisoft San Francisco"), [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), and [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") (now known as *X*) to base their tech offices and for their employees to live.[[112]](#cite_note-ega06a-115)

The final wave of technology materialized as AI companies have either been founded in the city or moved there attracted to a large talent pool, proximity to venture capital, and serendipitous connections.[[247]](#cite_note-255)

The [Sunset Reservoir](/wiki/Sunset_Reservoir "Sunset Reservoir") Solar Project, completed in 2010, installed 24,000 solar panels on the roof of the reservoir. The 5-megawatt plant more than tripled the city's 2-megawatt solar generation capacity when it opened in December 2010.[[248]](#cite_note-256)[[249]](#cite_note-257)

### Tourism and conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Tourism and conventions")]

See also: [Port of San Francisco](/wiki/Port_of_San_Francisco "Port of San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Fishermans_Wharf_Sign%2C_SF%2C_CA%2C_jjron_25.03.2012.jpg/250px-Fishermans_Wharf_Sign%2C_SF%2C_CA%2C_jjron_25.03.2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fishermans_Wharf_Sign,_SF,_CA,_jjron_25.03.2012.jpg)

The [Fisherman's Wharf](/wiki/Fisherman%27s_Wharf,_San_Francisco "Fisherman's Wharf, San Francisco") is a popular tourist attraction.

Tourism is one of San Francisco's most important private-sector industries, accounting for more than one out of seven jobs in the city.[[220]](#cite_note-Waters2009-228)[[250]](#cite_note-tourism-largest-private-sector-258) The city's [frequent portrayal](/wiki/San_Francisco_in_popular_culture "San Francisco in popular culture") in music, film, and popular culture has made the city and its landmarks recognizable worldwide. In 2016, it attracted the fifth-highest number of foreign tourists of any city in the United States.[[251]](#cite_note-259) In 2023, 23.1 million visitors arrived in San Francisco, adding US$9.3 billion to the economy.[[252]](#cite_note-260)
With a large hotel infrastructure and a major convention facility in the [Moscone Center](/wiki/Moscone_Center "Moscone Center"), San Francisco is a popular destination for annual conventions and conferences.[[253]](#cite_note-261)

Some of the most popular tourist attractions in San Francisco, as noted by the [Travel Channel](/wiki/Travel_Channel "Travel Channel"), include the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge") and [Alamo Square Park](/wiki/Alamo_Square,_San_Francisco "Alamo Square, San Francisco"), home to the famous "[Painted Ladies](/wiki/Painted_Ladies "Painted Ladies")". Both of these locations were often used as landscape shots for the hit American television sitcom *[Full House](/wiki/Full_House "Full House")*. There is also [Lombard Street](/wiki/Lombard_Street_(San_Francisco) "Lombard Street (San Francisco)"), known for its "crookedness" and extensive views. Tourists also visit [Pier 39](/wiki/Pier_39 "Pier 39"), which offers dining, shopping, entertainment, and views of the bay, sunbathing [California sea lions](/wiki/California_sea_lions "California sea lions"), the [Aquarium of the Bay](/wiki/Aquarium_of_the_Bay "Aquarium of the Bay"), and the famous [Alcatraz Island](/wiki/Alcatraz_Island "Alcatraz Island").[[254]](#cite_note-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Coit_Tower%2C_San_Francisco%2C_California_LCCN2010630427.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Coit_Tower%2C_San_Francisco%2C_California_LCCN2010630427.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coit_Tower,_San_Francisco,_California_LCCN2010630427.tif)

[Coit Tower](/wiki/Coit_Tower "Coit Tower") on [Telegraph Hill](/wiki/Telegraph_Hill,_San_Francisco "Telegraph Hill, San Francisco")

San Francisco also offers tourists varied nightlife in its neighborhoods.[[255]](#cite_note-263)[[256]](#cite_note-264)

The new Terminal Project at Pier 27 opened September 25, 2014, as a replacement for the old Pier 35.[[257]](#cite_note-265)

A heightened interest in conventioneering in San Francisco, marked by the establishment of convention centers such as Yerba Buena, acted as a feeder into the local tourist economy and resulted in an increase in the hotel industry: "In 1959, the city had fewer than thirty-three hundred first-class hotel rooms; by 1970, the number was nine thousand; and by 1999, there were more than thirty thousand."[[258]](#cite_note-266) The [commodification](/wiki/Pink_capitalism "Pink capitalism") of the [Castro District](/wiki/Castro_District,_San_Francisco "Castro District, San Francisco") has contributed to San Francisco's tourist economy.[[259]](#cite_note-267)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

Main article: [Culture of San Francisco](/wiki/Culture_of_San_Francisco "Culture of San Francisco")

See also: [San Francisco in popular culture](/wiki/San_Francisco_in_popular_culture "San Francisco in popular culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Palace_of_Fine_Arts_during_Schon_%E2%80%93_Holt_Salahi_wedding.jpg/250px-Palace_of_Fine_Arts_during_Schon_%E2%80%93_Holt_Salahi_wedding.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_of_Fine_Arts_during_Schon_%E2%80%93_Holt_Salahi_wedding.jpg)

The [Palace of Fine Arts](/wiki/Palace_of_Fine_Arts "Palace of Fine Arts"), originally built for the 1915 [Panama–Pacific International Exposition](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93Pacific_International_Exposition "Panama–Pacific International Exposition")

Although the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco"), [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square,_San_Francisco "Union Square, San Francisco"), and [Fisherman's Wharf](/wiki/Fisherman%27s_Wharf,_San_Francisco "Fisherman's Wharf, San Francisco") are well known around the world, San Francisco is also characterized by its numerous culturally rich streetscapes featuring [mixed-use](/wiki/Mixed-use_development "Mixed-use development") neighborhoods anchored around [central commercial corridors](/wiki/List_of_streets_in_San_Francisco "List of streets in San Francisco") to which residents and visitors alike can walk.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Because of these characteristics,[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*] San Francisco is ranked the "most walkable" city in the United States by [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score").[[260]](#cite_note-268)

Many neighborhoods feature a mix of businesses, restaurants and venues that cater to the daily needs of local residents while also serving many visitors and tourists. Some neighborhoods are dotted with boutiques, cafés and nightlife such as Union Street in [Cow Hollow](/wiki/Cow_Hollow,_San_Francisco "Cow Hollow, San Francisco"), 24th Street in [Noe Valley](/wiki/Noe_Valley,_San_Francisco "Noe Valley, San Francisco"), [Valencia Street](/wiki/Valencia_Street "Valencia Street") in the [Mission](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco"), Grant Avenue in [North Beach](/wiki/North_Beach,_San_Francisco "North Beach, San Francisco"), and Irving Street in the [Inner Sunset](/wiki/Sunset_District,_San_Francisco "Sunset District, San Francisco"). This approach especially has influenced the continuing South of Market neighborhood redevelopment with businesses and neighborhood services rising alongside high-rise residences.[[261]](#cite_note-FogDev-269)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Entering_the_Castro_district%2C_San_Francisco_%2831984322697%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Entering_the_Castro_district%2C_San_Francisco_%2831984322697%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entering_the_Castro_district,_San_Francisco_(31984322697)_(cropped).jpg)

[The Castro](/wiki/Castro_District,_San_Francisco "Castro District, San Francisco") is famous as one of the first [gay villages](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village") in the country.[[135]](#cite_note-SFGateCastro-139)

Since the 1990s, the demand for skilled [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") workers from local startups and nearby [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") has attracted [white-collar workers](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") from all over the world and created a high standard of living in San Francisco.[[262]](#cite_note-270) Many neighborhoods that were once [blue-collar](/wiki/Blue-collar "Blue-collar"), middle, and lower class have been [gentrifying](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), as many of the city's traditional business and industrial districts have experienced a renaissance driven by the redevelopment of the [Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)"), including the neighborhoods [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach,_San_Francisco "South Beach, San Francisco") and [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Francisco "Mission Bay, San Francisco"). The city's property values and household income have risen to among the highest in the nation,[[263]](#cite_note-271)[[264]](#cite_note-MedianIncome-272)[[265]](#cite_note-273) creating a large and upscale restaurant, retail, and entertainment scene. According to a 2014 quality of life survey of global cities, San Francisco has the [highest quality of living](/wiki/Most_livable_cities "Most livable cities") of any U.S. city.[[266]](#cite_note-274) However, due to the exceptionally high cost of living, many of the city's middle and lower-class families have been leaving the city for the outer suburbs of the [Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"), or for California's [Central Valley](/wiki/Central_Valley_(California) "Central Valley (California)").[[267]](#cite_note-MiddleClass-275) By June 2, 2015, the median rent was reported to be as high as $4,225.[[268]](#cite_note-276) The high cost of living is due in part to restrictive planning laws which limit new residential construction.[[269]](#cite_note-277)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/2019_San_Francisco_Carnaval_Grand_Parade_431_%2849490263633%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-2019_San_Francisco_Carnaval_Grand_Parade_431_%2849490263633%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2019_San_Francisco_Carnaval_Grand_Parade_431_(49490263633)_(cropped).jpg)

The [Mission District](/wiki/Mission_District,_San_Francisco "Mission District, San Francisco") is the historic center of the city's [Chicano](/wiki/Chicano "Chicano")/[Mexican-American](/wiki/Mexican-American "Mexican-American") population and greater [Hispanic and Latino](/wiki/Hispanics_and_Latinos_in_California "Hispanics and Latinos in California") community.

The international character that San Francisco has enjoyed since its founding is continued today by large numbers of immigrants from Asia and Latin America. With 39% of its residents born overseas,[[237]](#cite_note-ega06b-245) San Francisco has numerous neighborhoods filled with businesses and civic institutions catering to new arrivals. In particular, the arrival of many ethnic Chinese, which began to accelerate in the 1970s, has complemented the long-established community historically based in [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") throughout the city and has transformed the annual [Chinese New Year Parade](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chinese_New_Year_Festival_and_Parade "San Francisco Chinese New Year Festival and Parade") into the largest event of its kind on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With the arrival of the ["beat"](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation") writers and artists of the 1950s and societal changes culminating in the [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love") in the [Haight-Ashbury](/wiki/Haight-Ashbury "Haight-Ashbury") district during the 1960s, San Francisco became a center of [liberal](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States") activism and of the [counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") that arose at that time. The [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and to a lesser extent the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_(United_States) "Green Party (United States)") have dominated [city politics](/wiki/Politics_of_San_Francisco "Politics of San Francisco") since the late 1970s, after the [last serious](/wiki/John_Barbagelata "John Barbagelata") [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") challenger for city office [lost the 1975 mayoral election](/wiki/1975_San_Francisco_mayoral_election "1975 San Francisco mayoral election") by a narrow margin. San Francisco has not voted more than 20% for a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") presidential or senatorial candidate since [1988](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election").[[270]](#cite_note-278) In 2007, the city expanded its [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") and other [indigent](/wiki/Indigent "Indigent") medical programs into the [Healthy San Francisco](/wiki/Healthy_San_Francisco "Healthy San Francisco") program,[[271]](#cite_note-279) which [subsidizes](/wiki/Subsidy "Subsidy") certain medical services for eligible residents.[[272]](#cite_note-280)[[273]](#cite_note-281)[[274]](#cite_note-282)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/2017_SFMOMA_from_Yerba_Buena_Gardens.jpg/250px-2017_SFMOMA_from_Yerba_Buena_Gardens.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017_SFMOMA_from_Yerba_Buena_Gardens.jpg)

The [San Francisco Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/San_Francisco_Museum_of_Modern_Art "San Francisco Museum of Modern Art"), also known as SF MOMA

Since 1993, the San Francisco Department of Public Health has distributed 400,000 [free syringes](/wiki/Needle_and_syringe_programmes#San_Francisco "Needle and syringe programmes") every month aimed at reducing HIV and other health risks for drug users, as well as providing disposal sites and services.[[275]](#cite_note-283)[[276]](#cite_note-284)[[277]](#cite_note-285)

San Francisco also has had a very active environmental community. Starting with the founding of the [Sierra Club](/wiki/Sierra_Club "Sierra Club") in 1892 to the establishment of the non-profit [Friends of the Urban Forest](/wiki/Friends_of_the_Urban_Forest "Friends of the Urban Forest") in 1981, San Francisco has been at the forefront of many global discussions regarding the environment.[[278]](#cite_note-286)[[279]](#cite_note-287) The 1980 [San Francisco Recycling Program](/wiki/San_Francisco_Mandatory_Recycling_and_Composting_Ordinance "San Francisco Mandatory Recycling and Composting Ordinance") was one of the earliest curbside recycling programs.[[280]](#cite_note-288) The city's GoSolarSF incentive promotes solar installations and the [San Francisco Public Utilities Commission](/wiki/San_Francisco_Public_Utilities_Commission "San Francisco Public Utilities Commission") is rolling out the [CleanPowerSF](/wiki/CleanPowerSF "CleanPowerSF") program to sell electricity from local renewable sources.[[281]](#cite_note-289)[[282]](#cite_note-290) SF Greasecycle is a program to recycle used cooking oil for conversion to biodiesel.[[283]](#cite_note-291)

### LGBTQ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: LGBTQ")]

Main article: [LGBTQ culture in San Francisco](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_San_Francisco "LGBTQ culture in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/San_Francisco_Pride_Parade_2012-6.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Pride_Parade_2012-6.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Pride_Parade_2012-6.jpg)

[San Francisco Pride](/wiki/San_Francisco_Pride "San Francisco Pride") is one of the oldest and largest [LGBTQ pride](/wiki/Pride_(LGBTQ_culture) "Pride (LGBTQ culture)") events in the world.

San Francisco has long had an [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ_people "LGBTQ people")-friendly [history](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_San_Francisco "LGBTQ culture in San Francisco"). It was home to the first lesbian-rights organization in the United States, [Daughters of Bilitis](/wiki/Daughters_of_Bilitis "Daughters of Bilitis"); the first openly gay person to run for public office in the United States, [José Sarria](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Sarria "José Sarria"); the first openly gay man to be elected to public office in California, [Harvey Milk](/wiki/Harvey_Milk "Harvey Milk"); the first openly lesbian judge appointed in the U.S., [Mary C. Morgan](/wiki/Mary_C._Morgan "Mary C. Morgan"); and the first [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") police commissioner, [Theresa Sparks](/wiki/Theresa_Sparks "Theresa Sparks").[[284]](#cite_note-thetravelintern-292)

San Francisco was home to the first lesbian bar in America, Mona's, on Union Street. The bar aimed to stand out as a space where women could be served by women wearing tuxedos, playing to the exclusion of men in a sapphic space, and catered to local queer patrons instead of the tourist ideal. As the bar continued to see more clientele, the booming business relocated to its more permanent location at 440 Broadway. Here, the business underwent a name change – now [Mona's 440 Club](/wiki/Mona%27s_440_Club "Mona's 440 Club") – but continued hosting drag artists and various headliners. Most namely, [Gladys Bentley](/wiki/Gladys_Bentley "Gladys Bentley"), a drag king pioneer and performance artist,[[285]](#cite_note-293) quickly identified for her unofficial tuxedo and top hat attire. Despite being the first [lesbian bar](/wiki/Lesbian_bar "Lesbian bar") in the nation, it was eventually sold and rebranded under new management. Since its closing in the 1950s, other iconic sapphic bars in San Francisco have emerged, including [Wild Side West](/wiki/Wild_Side_West "Wild Side West"),[[286]](#cite_note-294) [Maud's](/wiki/Maud%27s_(bar) "Maud's (bar)"), and [The Lexington Club](/wiki/The_Lexington_Club "The Lexington Club"). More recently, Rikki's, the first and only women's sports bar, opened in Summer 2025.[[287]](#cite_note-295) Drag artists and live entertainment continue to be a thriving part of sapphic and queer spaces in the city and, more notably, [The Castro](/wiki/Castro_District,_San_Francisco "Castro District, San Francisco"). Today, sapphic spaces, even in San Francisco, continue struggling to keep their doors open and remain in decline in comparison to gay spaces.[[288]](#cite_note-296)

The city's large gay population has created and sustained a politically and culturally active community over many decades, developing a powerful presence in San Francisco's civic life.[[284]](#cite_note-thetravelintern-292) Survey data released in 2015 by [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") places the proportion of LGBTQ adults in the San Francisco metro area at 6.2%, which is the highest proportion of the 50 most populous metropolitan areas as measured by the polling organization.[[289]](#cite_note-297)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/The_Castro_%286163133524%29.jpg/250px-The_Castro_%286163133524%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Castro_(6163133524).jpg)

The [gay pride flag](/wiki/Rainbow_flag_(LGBTQ) "Rainbow flag (LGBTQ)") was originally developed in San Francisco.

One of the most popular destinations for gay tourists internationally, the city hosts [San Francisco Pride](/wiki/San_Francisco_Pride "San Francisco Pride"), one of the largest and oldest [pride parades](/wiki/Pride_parades "Pride parades"). San Francisco Pride events have been held continuously since 1972. The events are themed and a new theme is created each year.[[290]](#cite_note-298) In 2013, over 1.5 million people attended, around 500,000 more than the previous year.[[291]](#cite_note-299) [Pink Saturday](/wiki/Pink_Saturday "Pink Saturday") is an annual street party held the Saturday before the pride parade, which coincides with the [Dyke march](/wiki/Dyke_march "Dyke march").

The [Folsom Street Fair](/wiki/Folsom_Street_Fair "Folsom Street Fair") (FSF) is an annual [BDSM](/wiki/BDSM "BDSM") and [leather subculture](/wiki/Leather_subculture "Leather subculture") street fair that is held in September, endcapping San Francisco's "Leather Pride Week".[[292]](#cite_note-300) It started in 1984 and is California's third-largest single-day, outdoor spectator event and the world's largest leather event and showcase for BDSM products and culture.[[293]](#cite_note-301)

### Performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Performing arts")]

See also: [List of theatres in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_theatres_in_San_Francisco "List of theatres in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/San_Francisco_Opera_House_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Opera_House_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Opera_House_(cropped).jpg)

[War Memorial Opera House](/wiki/War_Memorial_Opera_House "War Memorial Opera House"), part of the [S.F. War Memorial & Performing Arts Center](/wiki/San_Francisco_War_Memorial_and_Performing_Arts_Center "San Francisco War Memorial and Performing Arts Center"), one of the largest performing arts centers in the U.S.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Golden_Gate_Theatre_-_San_Francisco%2C_CA_-_DSC06532_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Golden_Gate_Theatre_-_San_Francisco%2C_CA_-_DSC06532_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Golden_Gate_Theatre_-_San_Francisco,_CA_-_DSC06532_(cropped).jpg)

[Golden Gate Theatre](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Theatre "Golden Gate Theatre") is located in the historic [Theatre District](/wiki/Theatre_District,_San_Francisco "Theatre District, San Francisco")

San Francisco's [War Memorial and Performing Arts Center](/wiki/San_Francisco_War_Memorial_and_Performing_Arts_Center "San Francisco War Memorial and Performing Arts Center") hosts some of the most enduring performing arts companies in the country. The [War Memorial Opera House](/wiki/War_Memorial_Opera_House "War Memorial Opera House") houses the [San Francisco Opera](/wiki/San_Francisco_Opera "San Francisco Opera"), the second-largest opera company in North America[[294]](#cite_note-302) as well as the [San Francisco Ballet](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ballet "San Francisco Ballet"), while the [San Francisco Symphony](/wiki/San_Francisco_Symphony "San Francisco Symphony") plays in [Davies Symphony Hall](/wiki/Louise_M._Davies_Symphony_Hall "Louise M. Davies Symphony Hall"). Opened in 2013, the [SFJAZZ Center](/wiki/SFJAZZ_Center "SFJAZZ Center") hosts jazz performances year round.[[295]](#cite_note-303)

[The Fillmore](/wiki/The_Fillmore "The Fillmore") is a music venue located in the [Western Addition](/wiki/Western_Addition,_San_Francisco "Western Addition, San Francisco"). It is the second incarnation of the historic venue that gained fame in the 1960s, housing the stage where now-famous musicians such as the [Grateful Dead](/wiki/Grateful_Dead "Grateful Dead"), [Janis Joplin](/wiki/Janis_Joplin "Janis Joplin"), [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), and [Jefferson Airplane](/wiki/Jefferson_Airplane "Jefferson Airplane") first performed, fostering the [San Francisco Sound](/wiki/San_Francisco_Sound "San Francisco Sound").[[296]](#cite_note-304) It closed its doors in 1971 with a final performance by [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)") and reopened in 1994 with a show by [the Smashing Pumpkins](/wiki/The_Smashing_Pumpkins "The Smashing Pumpkins").[[297]](#cite_note-305)

San Francisco has a large number of [theaters and live performance venues](/wiki/List_of_theatres_in_San_Francisco "List of theatres in San Francisco"). Local theater companies have been noted for risk taking and innovation.[[298]](#cite_note-306) The [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award")-winning non-profit [American Conservatory Theater](/wiki/American_Conservatory_Theater "American Conservatory Theater") (A.C.T.) is a member of the national [League of Resident Theatres](/wiki/League_of_Resident_Theatres "League of Resident Theatres"). Other local winners of the [Regional Theatre Tony Award](/wiki/Regional_Theatre_Tony_Award "Regional Theatre Tony Award") include the [San Francisco Mime Troupe](/wiki/San_Francisco_Mime_Troupe "San Francisco Mime Troupe").[[299]](#cite_note-tbtb-307)
San Francisco theaters frequently host pre-[Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") engagements and tryout runs,[[300]](#cite_note-308) and some original San Francisco productions have later moved to Broadway.[[299]](#cite_note-tbtb-307)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Museums")]

Further information: [List of museums in San Francisco Bay Area, California § San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_San_Francisco_Bay_Area,_California#San_Francisco "List of museums in San Francisco Bay Area, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/California_Palace_of_the_Legion_of_Honor%2C_02.JPG/250px-California_Palace_of_the_Legion_of_Honor%2C_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:California_Palace_of_the_Legion_of_Honor,_02.JPG)

The [California Palace of the Legion of Honor](/wiki/Legion_of_Honor_(museum) "Legion of Honor (museum)"), part of the [Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco](/wiki/Fine_Arts_Museums_of_San_Francisco "Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco")

The [San Francisco Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/San_Francisco_Museum_of_Modern_Art "San Francisco Museum of Modern Art") (SFMOMA) houses 20th century and contemporary works of art. It moved to its current building in the [South of Market](/wiki/South_of_Market,_San_Francisco "South of Market, San Francisco") neighborhood in 1995 and attracted more than 600,000 visitors annually.[[301]](#cite_note-309) SFMOMA closed for renovation and expansion in 2013. The museum reopened in May 2016, with an addition, designed by [Snøhetta](/wiki/Sn%C3%B8hetta_(company) "Snøhetta (company)"), that has doubled the museum's size.[[302]](#cite_note-310)

The [Palace of the Legion of Honor](/wiki/California_Palace_of_the_Legion_of_Honor "California Palace of the Legion of Honor") holds primarily European antiquities and works of art at its [Lincoln Park](/wiki/Lincoln_Park_(San_Francisco) "Lincoln Park (San Francisco)") building modeled after its [Parisian namesake](/wiki/Palais_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Palais de la Légion d'Honneur"). The [de Young Museum](/wiki/M._H._de_Young_Memorial_Museum "M. H. de Young Memorial Museum") in Golden Gate Park features American decorative pieces and anthropological holdings from Africa, Oceania and the Americas, while Asian art is housed in the [Asian Art Museum](/wiki/Asian_Art_Museum_of_San_Francisco "Asian Art Museum of San Francisco"). Opposite the de Young stands the [California Academy of Sciences](/wiki/California_Academy_of_Sciences "California Academy of Sciences"), a natural history museum that also hosts the [Morrison Planetarium](/wiki/Morrison_Planetarium "Morrison Planetarium") and [Steinhart Aquarium](/wiki/Steinhart_Aquarium "Steinhart Aquarium").[[303]](#cite_note-311)

Located on Pier 15 on the Embarcadero, the [Exploratorium](/wiki/Exploratorium "Exploratorium") is an interactive science museum. The [Contemporary Jewish Museum](/wiki/Contemporary_Jewish_Museum "Contemporary Jewish Museum") is a non-collecting institution that hosts a broad array of temporary exhibitions. On Nob Hill, the [Cable Car Museum](/wiki/San_Francisco_Cable_Car_Museum "San Francisco Cable Car Museum") is a working museum featuring the cable car powerhouse, which drives the cables.[[304]](#cite_note-312) [Wattis Institute for Contemporary Arts](/wiki/Wattis_Institute_for_Contemporary_Arts "Wattis Institute for Contemporary Arts") was founded in 1998 and is part of the California College of the Arts.[[305]](#cite_note-313)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sports")]

Further information: [Sports in the San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/Sports_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "Sports in the San Francisco Bay Area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Oracle_Park_2021.jpg/250px-Oracle_Park_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oracle_Park_2021.jpg)

[Oracle Park](/wiki/Oracle_Park "Oracle Park"), home of the [SF Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants")

[Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") have played in San Francisco since moving from New York in 1958. The Giants play at [Oracle Park](/wiki/Oracle_Park "Oracle Park"), which opened in 2000.[[306]](#cite_note-314) The Giants won [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") titles in [2010](/wiki/2010_World_Series "2010 World Series"), [2012](/wiki/2012_World_Series "2012 World Series"), and in [2014](/wiki/2014_World_Series "2014 World Series"). The Giants have boasted stars such as [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays"), [Willie McCovey](/wiki/Willie_McCovey "Willie McCovey"), and [Barry Bonds](/wiki/Barry_Bonds "Barry Bonds") (MLB's [career home run leader](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_career_home_run_leaders "List of Major League Baseball career home run leaders")). In 2012, San Francisco was ranked No. 1 in a study that examined which U.S. metro areas have produced the most Major Leaguers since 1920.[[307]](#cite_note-315)

The [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) began playing in 1946 as an [All-America Football Conference](/wiki/All-America_Football_Conference "All-America Football Conference") (AAFC) league charter member, moved to the NFL in 1950 and into [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") in 1971. The team left San Francisco in 2014, moving approximately 50 miles south to [Santa Clara](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"), and began playing its home games at [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium"),[[308]](#cite_note-316)[[309]](#cite_note-317) Despite this, the 49ers are still branded as a San Francisco team, and when the team hosted [Super Bowl 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50") events were hosted in San Francisco such as the annual [NFL Experience](/wiki/National_Football_League_Experience "National Football League Experience") which was held at the [Moscone Center](/wiki/Moscone_Center "Moscone Center") and "Super Bowl City" which opened on January 30 at Justin Herman Plaza on [The Embarcadero](/wiki/Embarcadero_(San_Francisco) "Embarcadero (San Francisco)").[[310]](#cite_note-318)[[311]](#cite_note-319) The 49ers have won five [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") titles between 1982 and 1995.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Chase_Center_-_Warriors.jpg/250px-Chase_Center_-_Warriors.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chase_Center_-_Warriors.jpg)

[Chase Center](/wiki/Chase_Center "Chase Center"), home of the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors")

The NBA's Golden State Warriors have played in the San Francisco Bay Area since moving from Philadelphia in 1962. The Warriors played as the San Francisco Warriors, from 1962 to 1971, before being renamed the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") prior to the 1971–1972 season in an attempt to present the team as a representation of the whole state of California, which had already adopted "The Golden State" nickname.[[312]](#cite_note-320) The Warriors' arena, [Chase Center](/wiki/Chase_Center "Chase Center"), is located in San Francisco.[[313]](#cite_note-321) After winning two championships in Philadelphia, they have won five championships since moving to the San Francisco Bay Area,[[314]](#cite_note-322) and made five consecutive [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals") from 2015 to 2019, winning three of them. They won again in 2022, the franchise's first championship while residing in San Francisco proper.

At the collegiate level, the [San Francisco Dons](/wiki/San_Francisco_Dons "San Francisco Dons") compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"). [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") led the Dons basketball team to [NCAA championships](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") in 1955 and 1956. There is also the [San Francisco State Gators](/wiki/San_Francisco_State_Gators "San Francisco State Gators"), who compete in [NCAA Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II").[[315]](#cite_note-323) Oracle Park hosted the annual [Fight Hunger Bowl](/wiki/Fight_Hunger_Bowl "Fight Hunger Bowl") college football game from 2002 through 2013 before it moved to Santa Clara.

There are a handful of lower-league soccer clubs in San Francisco playing mostly from April – June.

| Club | Founded | Venue | League | Tier level |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [El Farolito](/wiki/El_Farolito "El Farolito") | 1985 | [Boxer Stadium](/wiki/Boxer_Stadium "Boxer Stadium") | [NPSL](/wiki/National_Professional_Soccer_League_(1967) "National Professional Soccer League (1967)") | 4 |
| [San Francisco City FC](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_FC "San Francisco City FC") | 2001 | [Kezar Stadium](/wiki/Kezar_Stadium "Kezar Stadium") | [USL League Two](/wiki/USL_League_Two "USL League Two") | 4 |
| [San Francisco Glens SC](/wiki/San_Francisco_Glens_SC "San Francisco Glens SC") | 1961 | [Skyline College](/wiki/Skyline_College "Skyline College") | [USL League Two](/wiki/USL_League_Two "USL League Two") | 4 |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Bay_to_Breakers_2024_%2853745753358%29.jpg/250px-Bay_to_Breakers_2024_%2853745753358%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bay_to_Breakers_2024_(53745753358).jpg)

[Bay to Breakers](/wiki/Bay_to_Breakers "Bay to Breakers") is an annual foot race known for colorful costumes.

The [Bay to Breakers](/wiki/Bay_to_Breakers "Bay to Breakers") footrace, held annually since 1912, is best known for colorful costumes and a celebratory community spirit.[[316]](#cite_note-324) The [San Francisco Marathon](/wiki/San_Francisco_Marathon "San Francisco Marathon") attracts more than 21,000 participants.[[317]](#cite_note-325) The [Escape from Alcatraz](/wiki/Escape_from_Alcatraz_(triathlon) "Escape from Alcatraz (triathlon)") [triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon") has, since 1980, attracted 2,000 top professional and amateur triathletes for its annual race.[[318]](#cite_note-326) The [Olympic Club](/wiki/Olympic_Club "Olympic Club"), founded in 1860, is the oldest [athletic club](/wiki/Sports_club "Sports club") in the United States. Its private golf course has hosted the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)") on five occasions. San Francisco hosted the [2013 America's Cup](/wiki/2013_America%27s_Cup "2013 America's Cup") yacht racing competition.[[319]](#cite_note-327)

With an ideal climate for outdoor activities, San Francisco has ample resources and opportunities for amateur and participatory sports and recreation. There are more than 200 miles (320 km) of [bicycle paths, lanes](/wiki/Segregated_cycle_facilities "Segregated cycle facilities") and bike routes in the city.[[320]](#cite_note-328)
San Francisco residents have often ranked among the fittest in the country.[[321]](#cite_note-fitness-329) [Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park") has miles of paved and unpaved running trails as well as a [golf course](/wiki/Golf_course "Golf course") and [disc golf](/wiki/Disc_golf "Disc golf") course.
Boating, sailing, [windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing") and [kitesurfing](/wiki/Kitesurfing "Kitesurfing") are among the popular activities on San Francisco Bay, and the city maintains a yacht harbor in the [Marina District](/wiki/Marina_District,_San_Francisco "Marina District, San Francisco").

San Francisco also has had [Esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") teams, such as the [Overwatch League](/wiki/Overwatch_League "Overwatch League")'s [San Francisco Shock](/wiki/San_Francisco_Shock "San Francisco Shock"). Established in 2017,[[322]](#cite_note-330) they won two back-to-back championship titles in 2019 and 2020.[[323]](#cite_note-331)[[324]](#cite_note-332)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

See also: [List of parks in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_San_Francisco "List of parks in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Aerial_view_of_San_Francisco%2C_30_Jun_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_San_Francisco%2C_30_Jun_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_San_Francisco,_30_Jun_2018_(cropped).jpg)

[Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park") is the 3rd most-visited [city park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") in the U.S., after [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") and the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall").[[325]](#cite_note-333)

Several of San Francisco's parks and nearly all of its beaches form part of the regional [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area"), one of the most visited units of the [National Park system](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") in the United States with over 13 million visitors a year. Among the GGNRA's attractions within the city are [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach,_San_Francisco "Ocean Beach, San Francisco"), which runs along the Pacific Ocean shoreline and is frequented by a [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") community, and [Baker Beach](/wiki/Baker_Beach "Baker Beach"), which is located in a cove west of the Golden Gate Bridge, as well as the [California Academy of Sciences](/wiki/California_Academy_of_Sciences "California Academy of Sciences"), a research institute and natural history museum.

The [Presidio of San Francisco](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") is the former 18th century Spanish military base, which today is one of the city's largest parks and home to numerous museums and institutions. Also within the Presidio is [Crissy Field](/wiki/Crissy_Field "Crissy Field"), a former airfield that was restored to its natural [salt marsh](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh") [ecosystem](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"). The GGNRA also administers [Fort Funston](/wiki/Fort_Funston "Fort Funston"), [Lands End](/wiki/Lands_End_(San_Francisco) "Lands End (San Francisco)"), [Fort Mason](/wiki/Fort_Mason "Fort Mason"), and [Alcatraz](/wiki/Alcatraz "Alcatraz"). The National Park Service separately administers the [San Francisco Maritime National Historical Park](/wiki/San_Francisco_Maritime_National_Historical_Park "San Francisco Maritime National Historical Park") – a fleet of historic ships and waterfront property around [Aquatic Park](/wiki/Aquatic_Park_Historic_District "Aquatic Park Historic District").[[326]](#cite_note-334)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Alamo_Square_with_Painted_Ladies%2C_SF%2C_CA%2C_jjron_26.03.2012.jpg/250px-Alamo_Square_with_Painted_Ladies%2C_SF%2C_CA%2C_jjron_26.03.2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alamo_Square_with_Painted_Ladies,_SF,_CA,_jjron_26.03.2012.jpg)

[Painted Ladies](/wiki/Painted_Ladies "Painted Ladies") on [Alamo Square](/wiki/Alamo_Square "Alamo Square").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Cliff_House_from_Ocean_Beach.jpg/250px-Cliff_House_from_Ocean_Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliff_House_from_Ocean_Beach.jpg)

The [Cliff House](/wiki/Cliff_House,_San_Francisco "Cliff House, San Francisco") over [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach,_San_Francisco "Ocean Beach, San Francisco")

There are more than [220 parks](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_San_Francisco "List of parks in San Francisco") maintained by the [San Francisco Recreation & Parks Department](/wiki/San_Francisco_Recreation_%26_Parks_Department "San Francisco Recreation & Parks Department").[[327]](#cite_note-335) The largest and best-known city park is [Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park"),[[328]](#cite_note-336) which stretches from the center of the city west to the Pacific Ocean. Once covered in native grasses and sand dunes, the park was conceived in the 1860s and was created by the extensive planting of thousands of non-native trees and plants. The large park is rich with cultural and natural attractions such as the [Conservatory of Flowers](/wiki/Conservatory_of_Flowers "Conservatory of Flowers"), [Japanese Tea Garden](/wiki/Japanese_Tea_Garden_(San_Francisco) "Japanese Tea Garden (San Francisco)") and [San Francisco Botanical Garden](/wiki/San_Francisco_Botanical_Garden "San Francisco Botanical Garden").[[329]](#cite_note-337) The [Dahlia](/wiki/Dahlia "Dahlia") is the official flower of San Francisco.

[Lake Merced](/wiki/Lake_Merced "Lake Merced") is a fresh-water lake surrounded by parkland[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[330]](#cite_note-338) and near the [San Francisco Zoo](/wiki/San_Francisco_Zoo "San Francisco Zoo"), a city-owned park that houses more than 250 animal species, many of which are endangered.[[331]](#cite_note-339) The only [park](/wiki/List_of_California_state_parks "List of California state parks") managed by the [California State Park](/wiki/California_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "California Department of Parks and Recreation") system located principally in San Francisco, [Candlestick Point](/wiki/Candlestick_Point_State_Recreation_Area "Candlestick Point State Recreation Area") was the state's first urban recreation area.[[332]](#cite_note-340)

Most of San Francisco's islands are protected as parkland or nature reserves. [Alcatraz Island](/wiki/Alcatraz_Island "Alcatraz Island"), operated by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), is open to the public.
