The [Farallon Islands](/wiki/Farallon_Islands "Farallon Islands") are protected wildlife refuges. The [Seal Rocks](/wiki/Seal_Rocks_(San_Francisco,_California) "Seal Rocks (San Francisco, California)") are protected as part of [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area"). [Red Rock Island](/wiki/Red_Rock_Island "Red Rock Island") is the only privately owned island in San Francisco Bay, but is uninhabited. [Yerba Buena Island](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Island "Yerba Buena Island") is largely utilized by the military.

San Francisco is the first city in the U.S. to have a park within a [10-Minute Walk](/wiki/10-Minute_Walk "10-Minute Walk") of every resident.[[333]](#cite_note-341)[[334]](#cite_note-342) It also ranks fifth in the U.S. for park access and quality in the 2018 ParkScore ranking of the top 100 park systems across the United States, according to the nonprofit [Trust for Public Land](/wiki/Trust_for_Public_Land "Trust for Public Land").[[335]](#cite_note-343)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Government")]

Main articles: [Government of San Francisco](/wiki/Government_of_San_Francisco "Government of San Francisco"), [Politics of San Francisco](/wiki/Politics_of_San_Francisco "Politics of San Francisco"), and [Mayors of San Francisco](/wiki/Mayor_of_San_Francisco "Mayor of San Francisco")

See also: [San Francisco City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall "San Francisco City Hall")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/San_Francisco_City_Hall_%28front%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_City_Hall_%28front%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_City_Hall_(front)_(cropped).jpg)

[San Francisco City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall "San Francisco City Hall"), built 1913–16 and designed by [Arthur Brown Jr.](/wiki/Arthur_Brown_Jr. "Arthur Brown Jr.")

The [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_San_Francisco "Mayor of San Francisco") is also the county executive, and the county [Board of Supervisors](/wiki/San_Francisco_Board_of_Supervisors "San Francisco Board of Supervisors") acts as the [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council"). The government of San Francisco is a [charter city](/wiki/Charter_city "Charter city") and is constituted of two co-equal branches: the executive branch is headed by the mayor and includes other citywide elected and appointed officials as well as the civil service; the 11-member Board of Supervisors, the legislative branch, is headed by a president and is responsible for passing laws and budgets, though San Franciscans also make use of [direct ballot initiatives](/wiki/Popular_initiative "Popular initiative") to pass legislation.[[336]](#cite_note-344)

Because of its unique city-county status, the local government is able to exercise jurisdiction over certain property outside city limits. [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport"), though located in [San Mateo County](/wiki/San_Mateo_County "San Mateo County"), is owned and operated by the City and County of San Francisco. San Francisco's largest jail complex (County Jail No. 5) is located in San Mateo County, in an [unincorporated area](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") adjacent to [San Bruno](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California"). San Francisco was also granted a perpetual leasehold over the [Hetch Hetchy Valley](/wiki/Hetch_Hetchy_Valley "Hetch Hetchy Valley") and [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") in [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park") by the [Raker Act](/wiki/Raker_Act "Raker Act") in 1913.[[337]](#cite_note-CityCounty-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Supremecourtofcaliforniamaincourthouse.jpg/250px-Supremecourtofcaliforniamaincourthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Supremecourtofcaliforniamaincourthouse.jpg)

The [Supreme Court of California](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_California "Supreme Court of California") is based in the [Earl Warren Building](/wiki/Earl_Warren_Building "Earl Warren Building").

The members of the Board of Supervisors are elected as representatives of specific districts within the city.[[338]](#cite_note-SF_Gov_Supervisor_Districts-346) Upon the death or resignation of the mayor, the President of the Board of Supervisors becomes acting mayor until the full Board elects an interim replacement for the remainder of the term. In 2011, [Ed Lee](/wiki/Ed_Lee_(politician) "Ed Lee (politician)") was selected by the board to finish the term of [Gavin Newsom](/wiki/Gavin_Newsom "Gavin Newsom"), who resigned to take office as [Lieutenant Governor of California](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_California "Lieutenant Governor of California").[[339]](#cite_note-347) Lee (who won two elections to remain mayor) was temporarily replaced by San Francisco Board of Supervisors President [London Breed](/wiki/London_Breed "London Breed") after he died on December 12, 2017. Supervisor [Mark Farrell](/wiki/Mark_Farrell_(politician) "Mark Farrell (politician)") was appointed by the Board of Supervisors to finish Lee's term on January 23, 2018.

Most local offices in San Francisco are elected using [ranked choice voting](/wiki/Ranked-choice_voting_in_the_United_States "Ranked-choice voting in the United States").[[340]](#cite_note-348)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/San_Francisco_Federal_Building_%28main_structure%2C_seen_from_Mission_and_7th%2C_2020%29.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Federal_Building_%28main_structure%2C_seen_from_Mission_and_7th%2C_2020%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Federal_Building_(main_structure,_seen_from_Mission_and_7th,_2020).jpg)

[San Francisco Federal Building](/wiki/San_Francisco_Federal_Building "San Francisco Federal Building")

San Francisco serves as the regional hub for many arms of the federal bureaucracy, including the [U.S. Court of Appeals](/wiki/Ninth_Circuit_U.S._Court_of_Appeals "Ninth Circuit U.S. Court of Appeals"), the [Federal Reserve Bank](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_San_Francisco "Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco"), and the [U.S. Mint](/wiki/United_States_Mint "United States Mint"). Until [decommissioning](/wiki/Base_Realignment_and_Closure "Base Realignment and Closure") in the early 1990s, the city had major military installations at the [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco"), [Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island,_San_Francisco "Treasure Island, San Francisco"), and [Hunters Point](/wiki/San_Francisco_Naval_Shipyard "San Francisco Naval Shipyard")—a legacy still reflected in the annual celebration of [Fleet Week](/wiki/Fleet_Week "Fleet Week"). The State of California uses San Francisco as the home of the [state supreme court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_California "Supreme Court of California") and other state agencies. Foreign governments maintain [more than seventy consulates in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "List of diplomatic missions in the San Francisco Bay Area").[[341]](#cite_note-349)

The municipal budget for fiscal year 2015–16 was $8.99 billion,[[342]](#cite_note-350) and is one of the largest city budgets in the United States.[[343]](#cite_note-ballotpedia.org-351) The City of San Francisco spends more per resident than any city other than Washington, D.C., over $10,000 in FY 2015–2016.[[343]](#cite_note-ballotpedia.org-351) The city employs around 27,000 workers.[[344]](#cite_note-352)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/U.S._Post_Office_%26_Courthouse_%28San_Francisco%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-U.S._Post_Office_%26_Courthouse_%28San_Francisco%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Post_Office_%26_Courthouse_(San_Francisco)_(cropped).jpg)

The historic [Browning Courthouse](/wiki/James_R._Browning_United_States_Court_of_Appeals_Building "James R. Browning United States Court of Appeals Building")

In the [California State Senate](/wiki/California_State_Senate "California State Senate"), San Francisco is in [the 11th senatorial district](/wiki/California%27s_11th_senatorial_district "California's 11th senatorial district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party") [Scott Wiener](/wiki/Scott_Wiener "Scott Wiener"). In the [California State Assembly](/wiki/California_State_Assembly "California State Assembly"), it is split between [the 17th Assembly district](/wiki/California%27s_17th_State_Assembly_district "California's 17th State Assembly district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party") [Matt Haney](/wiki/Matt_Haney "Matt Haney"), and [the 19th Assembly district](/wiki/California%27s_19th_State_Assembly_district "California's 19th State Assembly district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party") [Catherine Stefani](/wiki/Catherine_Stefani "Catherine Stefani").[[6]](#cite_note-CCRC_CoI-6)

In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), San Francisco is split between two congressional districts. Most of the city is in the [11th District](/wiki/California%27s_11th_congressional_district "California's 11th congressional district"), represented by [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")–[San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco,_California "San Francisco, California")). A sliver in the southwest is part of the [15th District](/wiki/California%27s_15th_congressional_district "California's 15th congressional district") represented by [Kevin Mullin](/wiki/Kevin_Mullin "Kevin Mullin") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")–[South San Francisco](/wiki/South_San_Francisco,_California "South San Francisco, California")).[[345]](#cite_note-353)

One of San Francisco's most notable achievements is its ambitious zero-waste goal, which aims to divert 100% of waste from landfills by 2025. The city has already made significant strides, with widespread recycling and composting programs that encourage residents to minimize waste. San Francisco was one of the first U.S. cities to ban plastic bags and continues to lead in banning single-use plastics, setting a standard for cities across the country.[[346]](#cite_note-354)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

See also: [List of colleges and universities in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_San_Francisco "List of colleges and universities in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Lone_Mountain_Campus_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lone_Mountain_Campus_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lone_Mountain_Campus_(cropped).jpg)

The [University of San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_San_Francisco "University of San Francisco")

The [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") is the sole campus of the [University of California system](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") entirely dedicated to graduate education in health and biomedical sciences. It is ranked among the top five medical schools in the United States[[347]](#cite_note-355) and operates the [UCSF Medical Center](/wiki/UCSF_Medical_Center "UCSF Medical Center"), which ranks as the number one hospital in California and the number 5 in the country.[[348]](#cite_note-356) UCSF is a major local employer, second in size only to the city and county government.[[349]](#cite_note-357)[[350]](#cite_note-358)[[351]](#cite_note-359) A 43-acre (17 ha) [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Francisco "Mission Bay, San Francisco") campus was opened in 2003, complementing its original facility in [Parnassus Heights](/wiki/List_of_hills_in_San_Francisco "List of hills in San Francisco"). It contains research space and facilities to foster biotechnology and life sciences entrepreneurship and will double the size of UCSF's research enterprise.[[352]](#cite_note-360) All in all, UCSF operates more than 20 facilities across San Francisco.[[353]](#cite_note-361)

The [University of California College of the Law, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco"), founded in [Civic Center](/wiki/Civic_Center,_San_Francisco "Civic Center, San Francisco") in 1878, is the oldest law school in California and claims more judges on the state bench than any other institution.[[354]](#cite_note-362)
San Francisco's two University of California institutions have recently formed an official affiliation in the UCSF/UC Law SF Consortium on Law, Science & Health Policy.[[355]](#cite_note-363)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/SFSU_Campus_Overview_Nov2012.JPG/250px-SFSU_Campus_Overview_Nov2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:SFSU_Campus_Overview_Nov2012.JPG)

The [San Francisco State University](/wiki/San_Francisco_State_University "San Francisco State University") library building

[San Francisco State University](/wiki/San_Francisco_State_University "San Francisco State University") is part of the [California State University](/wiki/California_State_University "California State University") system and is located near [Lake Merced](/wiki/Lake_Merced "Lake Merced").[[356]](#cite_note-sfsu-364) The school has approximately 30,000 students and awards undergraduate, master's and doctoral degrees in more than 100 disciplines.[[356]](#cite_note-sfsu-364) The [City College of San Francisco](/wiki/City_College_of_San_Francisco "City College of San Francisco"), with its main facility in the Ingleside district, is one of the largest two-year [community colleges](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") in the country. It has an enrollment of about 100,000 students and offers an extensive continuing education program.[[357]](#cite_note-365)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/McAllister_Tower_in_San_Francisco_%28TK2%29.JPG/250px-McAllister_Tower_in_San_Francisco_%28TK2%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:McAllister_Tower_in_San_Francisco_(TK2).JPG)

The [University of California College of the Law](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco")

Founded in 1855, the [University of San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_San_Francisco "University of San Francisco"), a private [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") university located on [Lone Mountain](/wiki/Lone_Mountain_(California) "Lone Mountain (California)"), is the oldest institution of higher education in San Francisco and one of the oldest universities established west of the Mississippi River.[[358]](#cite_note-366) [Golden Gate University](/wiki/Golden_Gate_University "Golden Gate University") is a private, nonsectarian university formed in 1901 and located in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_San_Francisco "Financial District, San Francisco").

[California Institute of Integral Studies](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Integral_Studies "California Institute of Integral Studies"), founded in 1968, offers graduate programs in its Schools of Professional Psychology & Health, and Consciousness and Transformation.

With an enrollment of 13,000 students, the [Academy of Art University](/wiki/Academy_of_Art_University "Academy of Art University") is the largest institute of art and design in the nation.[[359]](#cite_note-367) The [California College of the Arts](/wiki/California_College_of_the_Arts "California College of the Arts"), located north of [Potrero Hill](/wiki/Potrero_Hill "Potrero Hill"), has programs in architecture, fine arts, design, and writing.[[360]](#cite_note-368) The [San Francisco Conservatory of Music](/wiki/San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music "San Francisco Conservatory of Music"), the only independent [music school](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") on the West Coast, grants degrees in orchestral instruments, chamber music, composition, and conducting. The [San Francisco Art Institute](/wiki/San_Francisco_Art_Institute "San Francisco Art Institute"), founded in 1871 and the oldest [art school](/wiki/Art_school "Art school") west of the Mississippi,[[361]](#cite_note-369) closed in 2022.

The [California Culinary Academy](/wiki/California_Culinary_Academy "California Culinary Academy") had programs in the culinary arts, baking and pastry arts, and hospitality and restaurant management, and is now closed.

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

See also: [San Francisco public grammar schools](/wiki/San_Francisco_public_grammar_schools "San Francisco public grammar schools") and [List of high schools in California § San Francisco County](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_California#San_Francisco_County "List of high schools in California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Mission_High_School.jpg/250px-Mission_High_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mission_High_School.jpg)

The [San Francisco Unified School District](/wiki/San_Francisco_Unified_School_District "San Francisco Unified School District") operates 114 schools and is the oldest school district in California.

[Public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(government_funded) "Public school (government funded)") are run by the [San Francisco Unified School District](/wiki/San_Francisco_Unified_School_District "San Francisco Unified School District"), which covers the entire city and county,[[362]](#cite_note-370) as well as the [California State Board of Education](/wiki/California_State_Board_of_Education "California State Board of Education") for some charter schools. [Lowell High School](/wiki/Lowell_High_School_(San_Francisco) "Lowell High School (San Francisco)"), the oldest public high school in the U.S. west of the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"),[[363]](#cite_note-371) and the smaller [School of the Arts High School](/wiki/Ruth_Asawa_San_Francisco_School_of_the_Arts "Ruth Asawa San Francisco School of the Arts") are two of San Francisco's [magnet schools](/wiki/Magnet_school "Magnet school") at the secondary level. Public school students attend schools based on an assignment system rather than neighborhood proximity.[[364]](#cite_note-372)

Just under 30% of the city's school-age population attends one of San Francisco's more than 100 [private](/wiki/Private_school "Private school") or [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_school "Parochial school"), compared to a 10% rate nationwide.[[365]](#cite_note-SFGPrvtSchools-373) Nearly 40 of those schools are [Catholic schools](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") managed by the [Archdiocese of San Francisco](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_San_Francisco "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Francisco").[[366]](#cite_note-374)

San Francisco has nearly 300 [preschool](/wiki/Preschool "Preschool") programs primarily operated by [Head Start](/wiki/Head_Start_(program) "Head Start (program)"), [San Francisco Unified School District](/wiki/San_Francisco_Unified_School_District "San Francisco Unified School District"), private for-profit, private non-profit and [family child care](/wiki/Child_care#Family_child_care_homes "Child care") providers.[[367]](#cite_note-375) All four-year-old children living in San Francisco are offered [universal access to preschool](/wiki/Universal_preschool "Universal preschool").[[368]](#cite_note-376)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/San_Francisco_Chronicle_2023.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Chronicle_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Chronicle_2023.jpg)

The [San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle") building

The major daily newspaper in San Francisco is the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")*, which is currently Northern California's most widely circulated newspaper.[[369]](#cite_note-377) The Chronicle is most famous for a former columnist, the late [Herb Caen](/wiki/Herb_Caen "Herb Caen"), whose daily musings attracted critical acclaim and represented the "voice of San Francisco". *[The San Francisco Examiner](/wiki/The_San_Francisco_Examiner "The San Francisco Examiner")*, once the cornerstone of [William Randolph Hearst](/wiki/William_Randolph_Hearst "William Randolph Hearst")'s media empire and the home of [Ambrose Bierce](/wiki/Ambrose_Bierce "Ambrose Bierce"), declined in circulation over the years and now takes the form of a free daily tabloid, under new ownership.[[370]](#cite_note-378)[[371]](#cite_note-379)

*[Sing Tao Daily](/wiki/Sing_Tao_Daily "Sing Tao Daily")* claims to be the largest of several Chinese language dailies that serve the Bay Area.[[372]](#cite_note-380) *[SF Weekly](/wiki/SF_Weekly "SF Weekly")* is the city's [alternative weekly](/wiki/Alternative_weekly "Alternative weekly") newspaper. *[San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_(magazine) "San Francisco (magazine)")* and *[7x7](/wiki/7x7_(magazine) "7x7 (magazine)")* are major glossy magazines about San Francisco. The national newsmagazine *[Mother Jones](/wiki/Mother_Jones_(magazine) "Mother Jones (magazine)")* is also based in San Francisco. San Francisco is home to online-only media publications such as [SFist](/wiki/SFist "SFist"), and *[AsianWeek](/wiki/AsianWeek "AsianWeek")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Hearst_Building_entrance_-_San_Francisco%2C_CA_-_DSC06547.jpg/250px-Hearst_Building_entrance_-_San_Francisco%2C_CA_-_DSC06547.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hearst_Building_entrance_-_San_Francisco,_CA_-_DSC06547.jpg)

The [Julia Morgan](/wiki/Julia_Morgan "Julia Morgan")-designed Hearst Building, the western headquarters of the [Hearst Corporation](/wiki/Hearst_Corporation "Hearst Corporation")

The San Francisco Bay Area is the sixth-largest [television market](/wiki/Designated_market_area "Designated market area") in the U.S.[[373]](#cite_note-381) It is the fourth-largest [radio market](/wiki/Designated_market_area "Designated market area") after that of New York City, Los Angeles, and Chicago.[[374]](#cite_note-382)

All major U.S. television networks have [affiliates](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "List of television stations in the San Francisco Bay Area") serving the region, with most of them based in the city. [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [MSNBC](/wiki/MSNBC "MSNBC"), [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), [Russia Today](/wiki/RT_(TV_network) "RT (TV network)"), and [CCTV America](/wiki/CCTV_America "CCTV America") also have regional news bureaus in San Francisco. [Bloomberg West](/wiki/Bloomberg_West "Bloomberg West") was launched in 2011 from a studio on the Embarcadero and [CNBC](/wiki/CNBC "CNBC") broadcasts from [One Market Plaza](/wiki/One_Market_Plaza "One Market Plaza") since 2015. [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") uses the local ABC studio for their broadcasting. The [regional sports network](/wiki/Regional_sports_network "Regional sports network"), [Comcast SportsNet Bay Area](/wiki/Comcast_SportsNet_Bay_Area "Comcast SportsNet Bay Area") and its sister station [Comcast SportsNet California](/wiki/Comcast_SportsNet_California "Comcast SportsNet California"), are both located in San Francisco. The [Pac-12 Network](/wiki/Pac-12_Network "Pac-12 Network") is also based in San Francisco.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Sutro_Tower_from_Grandview.jpg/250px-Sutro_Tower_from_Grandview.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sutro_Tower_from_Grandview.jpg)

[Sutro Tower](/wiki/Sutro_Tower "Sutro Tower") is a [broadcast tower](/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers "Radio masts and towers") and local landmark.

[Public broadcasting](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") outlets include both a [television station](/wiki/KQED-TV "KQED-TV") and a [radio station](/wiki/KQED-FM "KQED-FM"), both broadcasting under the call letters KQED from a facility near the [Potrero Hill](/wiki/Potrero_Hill "Potrero Hill") neighborhood. KQED-FM is the most-listened-to [National Public Radio](/wiki/National_Public_Radio "National Public Radio") affiliate in the country.[[375]](#cite_note-383)

[KUSF](/wiki/KUSF_(University_of_San_Francisco) "KUSF (University of San Francisco)") is a student-run radio station by college students from the [University of San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_San_Francisco "University of San Francisco").[[376]](#cite_note-384) Another local broadcaster, [KPOO](/wiki/KPOO "KPOO"), is an independent, African-American owned and operated noncommercial radio station established in 1971.[[377]](#cite_note-385) [CNET](/wiki/CNET_Networks "CNET Networks"), founded 1994, and [Salon.com](/wiki/Salon.com "Salon.com"), 1995, are based in San Francisco. [Sutro Tower](/wiki/Sutro_Tower "Sutro Tower") is an important [broadcast tower](/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers "Radio masts and towers") located between [Mount Sutro](/wiki/Mount_Sutro "Mount Sutro") and the [Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_(San_Francisco) "Twin Peaks (San Francisco)"), built in 1973 for [KTVU](/wiki/KTVU "KTVU"), [KRON](/wiki/KRON "KRON"), and [KPIX](/wiki/KPIX "KPIX").

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transportation")]

See also: [Transportation in the San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/Transportation_in_the_San_Francisco_Bay_Area "Transportation in the San Francisco Bay Area")

#### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

See also: [San Francisco Municipal Railway](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Railway "San Francisco Municipal Railway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Cable_Car_No._1_and_Alcatraz_Island.jpg/250px-Cable_Car_No._1_and_Alcatraz_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cable_Car_No._1_and_Alcatraz_Island.jpg)

A [San Francisco cable car](/wiki/San_Francisco_cable_car_system "San Francisco cable car system") with [Alcatraz](/wiki/Alcatraz "Alcatraz") seen behind

Transit is the most used form of transportation every day in San Francisco. Every weekday, more than 560,000 people travel on Muni's 69 bus routes and more than 140,000 customers ride the Muni Metro light rail system.[[378]](#cite_note-:0-386) 32% of San Francisco residents use public transportation for their daily commute to work, ranking it fourth in the United States and first on the West Coast.[[379]](#cite_note-387) The [San Francisco Municipal Railway](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Railway "San Francisco Municipal Railway"), primarily known as Muni, is the primary public transit system of San Francisco. As of 2023, Muni is the eighth-largest transit system in the United States.[[380]](#cite_note-388)

The system operates a combined [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") and [heavy rail](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") (subway) system, the [Muni Metro](/wiki/Muni_Metro "Muni Metro"), as well as large bus and [trolley coach](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_San_Francisco "Trolleybuses in San Francisco") networks.[[381]](#cite_note-transportation-389) Additionally, it runs a [historic streetcar line](/wiki/F_Market "F Market"), which runs on Market Street from [Castro Street](/wiki/The_Castro "The Castro") to [Fisherman's Wharf](/wiki/Fisherman%27s_Wharf,_San_Francisco "Fisherman's Wharf, San Francisco").[[381]](#cite_note-transportation-389) It also operates the famous [cable cars](/wiki/San_Francisco_cable_car_system "San Francisco cable car system"),[[381]](#cite_note-transportation-389) which have been designated as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") and are a major tourist attraction.[[382]](#cite_note-390)

[Bay Area Rapid Transit](/wiki/Bay_Area_Rapid_Transit "Bay Area Rapid Transit") (BART), a regional Rapid Transit system, connects San Francisco with the [East Bay](/wiki/East_Bay_(San_Francisco_Bay_Area) "East Bay (San Francisco Bay Area)") and San Jose through the underwater [Transbay Tube](/wiki/Transbay_Tube "Transbay Tube"). The line, which contains all except the Orange Line, runs under Market Street to [Civic Center](/wiki/Civic_Center,_San_Francisco "Civic Center, San Francisco") where it turns south to the Mission District, the southern part of the city, and through northern [San Mateo County](/wiki/San_Mateo_County,_California "San Mateo County, California"), to the [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport"), and [Millbrae](/wiki/Millbrae,_California "Millbrae, California").[[381]](#cite_note-transportation-389) BART also shares stations with SFMTA [Muni Metro](/wiki/Muni_Metro "Muni Metro") under [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(San_Francisco) "Market Street (San Francisco)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/L_Taraval_train_on_Ulloa_Street%2C_June_2017-001_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-L_Taraval_train_on_Ulloa_Street%2C_June_2017-001_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:L_Taraval_train_on_Ulloa_Street,_June_2017-001_(cropped).JPG)

[Muni Metro](/wiki/Muni_Metro "Muni Metro"), run by [SF Muni](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Railway "San Francisco Municipal Railway")

Another commuter rail system, [Caltrain](/wiki/Caltrain "Caltrain"), runs from San Francisco along the [San Francisco Peninsula](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peninsula "San Francisco Peninsula") to [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California").[[381]](#cite_note-transportation-389) Historically, trains operated by [Southern Pacific Lines](/wiki/Southern_Pacific_Lines "Southern Pacific Lines") ran from San Francisco to Los Angeles, via [Palo Alto](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California") and [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California").

[Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway") runs a shuttle bus from three locations in San Francisco to its station across the bay in [Emeryville](/wiki/Emeryville,_California "Emeryville, California").[[383]](#cite_note-391) Additionally, BART offers connections to San Francisco from Amtrak's stations in Emeryville, Oakland and [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_California "Richmond, California"), and Caltrain offers connections in San Jose and [Santa Clara](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"). Thruway service also runs south to [San Luis Obispo](/wiki/San_Luis_Obispo,_California "San Luis Obispo, California") with connection to the [Pacific Surfliner](/wiki/Pacific_Surfliner "Pacific Surfliner").

San Francisco was an early adopter of [carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") in America. The non-profit [City CarShare](/wiki/City_CarShare "City CarShare") opened in 2001[[384]](#cite_note-392) and [Zipcar](/wiki/Zipcar "Zipcar") closely followed.[[385]](#cite_note-393)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/The_Larkspur_Ferry_%E2%80%9CMendocino%E2%80%9D_at_the_San_Francisco_Ferry_Terminal_in_June_of_2023_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-The_Larkspur_Ferry_%E2%80%9CMendocino%E2%80%9D_at_the_San_Francisco_Ferry_Terminal_in_June_of_2023_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Larkspur_Ferry_%E2%80%9CMendocino%E2%80%9D_at_the_San_Francisco_Ferry_Terminal_in_June_of_2023_(cropped).jpg)

[Golden Gate Ferries](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Ferry "Golden Gate Ferry") connect the city to [North Bay](/wiki/North_Bay_(San_Francisco_Bay_Area) "North Bay (San Francisco Bay Area)") communities, while [San Francisco Bay Ferry](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Ferry "San Francisco Bay Ferry") connects the city to both the North and [East Bay](/wiki/East_Bay "East Bay").

[San Francisco Bay Ferry](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Ferry "San Francisco Bay Ferry") operates from the [Ferry Building](/wiki/San_Francisco_Ferry_Building "San Francisco Ferry Building") and [Pier 39](/wiki/Pier_39 "Pier 39") to points in [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland_Ferry_Terminal "Oakland Ferry Terminal"), [Alameda](/wiki/Alameda,_California "Alameda, California"), [Bay Farm Island](/wiki/Bay_Farm_Island,_Alameda,_California "Bay Farm Island, Alameda, California"), [South San Francisco](/wiki/South_San_Francisco_Ferry_Terminal "South San Francisco Ferry Terminal"), [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond_Ferry_Terminal "Richmond Ferry Terminal"), and north to [Vallejo](/wiki/Vallejo,_California "Vallejo, California") in [Solano County](/wiki/Solano_County,_California "Solano County, California").[[386]](#cite_note-394) The [Golden Gate Ferry](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Ferry "Golden Gate Ferry") is the other ferry operator with service between San Francisco and [Marin County](/wiki/Marin_County "Marin County").[[387]](#cite_note-395) [SolTrans](/wiki/SolTrans "SolTrans") runs supplemental bus service between the Ferry Building and Vallejo.

To accommodate the large amount of San Francisco citizens who commute to the [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") daily, employers like [Genentech](/wiki/Genentech "Genentech"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), and [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") have begun to provide private bus transportation for their employees, from San Francisco locations. These buses have quickly become a heated topic of debate within the city, as [protesters](/wiki/San_Francisco_tech_bus_protests "San Francisco tech bus protests") claim they block bus lanes and delay public buses.[[388]](#cite_note-396)

#### Freeways and roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Freeways and roads")]

Further information: [List of streets in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_streets_in_San_Francisco "List of streets in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Bay_Bridge_2022.jpg/250px-Bay_Bridge_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bay_Bridge_2022.jpg)

The [Bay Bridge](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") connects the city to [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California") and the East Bay.

In 2014, only 41.3% of residents commuted by driving alone or carpooling in private vehicles in San Francisco, a decline from 48.6% in 2000.[[389]](#cite_note-:5-397) There are 1,088 miles of streets in San Francisco with 946 miles of these streets being surface streets, and 59 miles of freeways.[[389]](#cite_note-:5-397) Due to its unique geography, and the [freeway revolts](/wiki/Highway_revolts_in_the_United_States#San_Francisco "Highway revolts in the United States") of the late 1950s,[[390]](#cite_note-freeway-398)
[I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_California "Interstate 80 in California") begins at the approach to the [Bay Bridge](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") and is the only direct automobile link to the East Bay. [US 101](/wiki/U.S._Route_101_in_California "U.S. Route 101 in California") connects to the western terminus of I-80 and provides access to the south of the city along San Francisco Bay toward [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley"). Northward, the routing for US 101 uses arterial streets to connect to the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge"), the only direct automobile link to [Marin County](/wiki/Marin_County,_California "Marin County, California") and the North Bay.

As part of the retrofitting of the Golden Gate Bridge and installation of a suicide barrier, starting in 2019 the railings on the west side of the pedestrian walkway were replaced with thinner, more flexible [slats](/wiki/Slat_(aircraft) "Slat (aircraft)") in order to improve the bridge's aerodynamic tolerance of high wind to 100 mph (161 km/h). Starting in June 2020, reports were received of a loud hum produced by the new railing slats, heard across the city when a strong west wind was blowing.[[391]](#cite_note-bridge_harmonic_resonance_issues-399)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Lombard_Street_2020.jpg/250px-Lombard_Street_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lombard_Street_2020.jpg)

[Lombard Street](/wiki/Lombard_Street_(San_Francisco) "Lombard Street (San Francisco)") in [Russian Hill](/wiki/Russian_Hill,_San_Francisco "Russian Hill, San Francisco") is famed as "the most crooked street in the world".

[SR 1](/wiki/California_State_Route_1 "California State Route 1") also enters San Francisco from the north via the Golden Gate Bridge and bisects the city as the [19th Avenue](/wiki/19th_Avenue_(San_Francisco) "19th Avenue (San Francisco)") arterial thoroughfare, joining with [I-280](/wiki/Interstate_280_(California) "Interstate 280 (California)") at the city's southern border. I-280 continues south from San Francisco, and also turns to the east along the southern edge of the city, terminating just south of the Bay Bridge in the [South of Market](/wiki/South_of_Market_(San_Francisco) "South of Market (San Francisco)") neighborhood. After the [1989 Loma Prieta earthquake](/wiki/1989_Loma_Prieta_earthquake "1989 Loma Prieta earthquake"), city leaders demolished the [Embarcadero Freeway](/wiki/California_State_Route_480 "California State Route 480") and a portion of the [Central Freeway](/wiki/Central_Freeway "Central Freeway"), converting them into street-level boulevards.[[390]](#cite_note-freeway-398)

[SR 35](/wiki/California_State_Route_35 "California State Route 35") enters the city from the south as [Skyline Boulevard](/wiki/California_State_Route_35 "California State Route 35") and terminates at its intersection of Highway 1. [SR 82](/wiki/California_State_Route_82 "California State Route 82") enters San Francisco from the south as [Mission Street](/wiki/Mission_Street "Mission Street"), and terminates shortly thereafter at its intersection of I-280. The western terminus of the historic transcontinental [Lincoln Highway](/wiki/Lincoln_Highway "Lincoln Highway"), the first road across America, is in San Francisco's [Lincoln Park](/wiki/Lincoln_Park_(San_Francisco) "Lincoln Park (San Francisco)").

##### Vision Zero

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Vision Zero")]

In 2014, San Francisco committed to [Vision Zero](/wiki/Vision_Zero "Vision Zero"), with the goal of ending all traffic fatalities caused by motor vehicles within the city by 2024.[[392]](#cite_note-400) San Francisco's Vision Zero plan calls for investing in engineering, enforcement, and education, and focusing on dangerous intersections. In 2013, 25 people were killed by car and truck drivers while walking and biking in the city and 9 car drivers and passengers were killed in collisions. In 2019, 42 people were killed in traffic collisions in San Francisco.[[393]](#cite_note-401)

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Airports")]

Main article: [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/San_Francisco_International_Airport_-_aerial_photo.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_International_Airport_-_aerial_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_International_Airport_-_aerial_photo.jpg)

[San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport"), one of the [busiest airports in the world](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic")

Though located 13 miles (21 km) south of downtown in [unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") [San Mateo County](/wiki/San_Mateo_County,_California "San Mateo County, California"), [San Francisco International Airport](/wiki/San_Francisco_International_Airport "San Francisco International Airport") (SFO) is under the jurisdiction of the City and County of San Francisco. SFO is a hub for [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines")[[394]](#cite_note-UAL-402) and [Alaska Airlines](/wiki/Alaska_Airlines "Alaska Airlines").[[395]](#cite_note-403) SFO is a major international gateway to Asia and Europe, with the largest international terminal in North America.[[396]](#cite_note-intlterminalfactsheet-404) In 2011, SFO was the eighth-busiest airport in the U.S. and the 22nd-busiest in the world, handling over 40.9 million passengers.[[397]](#cite_note-SFO-stats-2011-405)

Located in the [South Bay](/wiki/Santa_Clara_Valley "Santa Clara Valley"), the [San Jose International Airport](/wiki/San_Jose_International_Airport "San Jose International Airport") (SJC) is the second-busiest airport in the Bay Area, followed by [Oakland San Francisco Bay Airport](/wiki/Oakland_San_Francisco_Bay_Airport "Oakland San Francisco Bay Airport"), which is a popular, low-cost alternative to SFO.

#### Cycling and walking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Cycling and walking")]

Main article: [Cycling in San Francisco](/wiki/Cycling_in_San_Francisco "Cycling in San Francisco")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/San_Francisco_Bay_Wheels.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Bay_Wheels.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Bay_Wheels.jpg)

A [Bay Wheels](/wiki/Bay_Wheels "Bay Wheels") station on [Market St.](/wiki/Market_Street_(San_Francisco) "Market Street (San Francisco)")

Cycling is a popular mode of transportation in San Francisco, with 75,000 residents commuting by bicycle each day.[[398]](#cite_note-2011_Bicycle_Count_Report-406) In recent years,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the city has installed better [cycling infrastructure](/wiki/Cycling_infrastructure "Cycling infrastructure") such as [protected bike lanes](/wiki/Protected_bike_lane "Protected bike lane") and parking racks.[[399]](#cite_note-407) [Bay Wheels](/wiki/Bay_Wheels "Bay Wheels"), previously named Bay Area Bike Share at inception, launched in August 2013 with 700 bikes in downtown San Francisco, selected cities in the East Bay, and San Jose. The [San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Transportation_Agency "San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency") and [Bay Area Air Quality Management District](/wiki/Bay_Area_Air_Quality_Management_District "Bay Area Air Quality Management District") are responsible for the operation with management provided by [Motivate](/wiki/Motivate_(company) "Motivate (company)").[[400]](#cite_note-408) A major expansion started in 2017, along with a rebranding as Ford GoBike; the company received its current name in 2019.[[401]](#cite_note-409)
Pedestrian traffic is also widespread. In 2021, [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") ranked San Francisco the most walkable city in the United States.[[402]](#cite_note-410)[[403]](#cite_note-411)[[404]](#cite_note-412)

San Francisco has significantly higher rates of pedestrian and bicyclist traffic deaths than the United States on average. In 2013, 21 pedestrians were killed in vehicle collisions, the highest since 2001,[[405]](#cite_note-SF_Examiner_ped_safety-413) which is 2.5 deaths per 100,000 population – 70% higher than the national average of 1.5.[[406]](#cite_note-NHTSA_2012_Crashes-414)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Critical_Mass%2C_San_Francisco%2C_April_29%2C_2005_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Critical_Mass%2C_San_Francisco%2C_April_29%2C_2005_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Critical_Mass,_San_Francisco,_April_29,_2005_(cropped).jpg)

A [San Francisco cycling](/wiki/Cycling_in_San_Francisco "Cycling in San Francisco") event

[Cycling](/wiki/Cycling_in_San_Francisco "Cycling in San Francisco") is becoming increasingly popular in the city. The 2010 [Municipal Transportation Agency (MTA)](/wiki/San_Francisco_Municipal_Transportation_Agency "San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency") annual bicycle count showed the number of cyclists at 33 locations had increased 58% from the 2006 baseline counts.[[407]](#cite_note-415) In 2008, the MTA estimated that about 128,000 trips were made by bicycle each day in the city, or 6% of total trips.[[408]](#cite_note-416)

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit), 2.6% of the city's streets have protected bike lanes, with 28 miles of protected bike lanes in the city.[[378]](#cite_note-:0-386) Since 2006, San Francisco has received a Bicycle Friendly Community status of "Gold" from the [League of American Bicyclists](/wiki/League_of_American_Bicyclists "League of American Bicyclists").[[409]](#cite_note-417) In 2022 a measure on the ballot passed to protect JFK drive in Golden Gate Park as a pedestrian and biking space with 59% of voters in favor.[[410]](#cite_note-418)

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Public safety")]

See also: [History of the San Francisco Police Department](/wiki/History_of_the_San_Francisco_Police_Department "History of the San Francisco Police Department")

The [San Francisco Police Department](/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department "San Francisco Police Department") was founded in 1849.[[411]](#cite_note-419) The portions of the [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area") located within the city, including the [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Francisco "Presidio of San Francisco") and [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach,_San_Francisco "Ocean Beach, San Francisco"), are patrolled by the [United States Park Police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police").

The [San Francisco Fire Department](/wiki/San_Francisco_Fire_Department "San Francisco Fire Department") provides both fire suppression and emergency medical services to the city.[[412]](#cite_note-420)

## Sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Sister cities")]

Main articles: [Sister cities of San Francisco, California](/wiki/Sister_cities_of_San_Francisco,_California "Sister cities of San Francisco, California") and [List of diplomatic missions in San Francisco](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_San_Francisco "List of diplomatic missions in San Francisco")

San Francisco participates in the [Sister Cities](/wiki/Sister_Cities_International "Sister Cities International") program.[[413]](#cite_note-421) A total of 41 consulates general and 23 honorary consulates have offices in the San Francisco Bay Area.[[414]](#cite_note-422)

In January 1980, Mayor [Dianne Feinstein](/wiki/Dianne_Feinstein "Dianne Feinstein") signed a sister cities agreement with Shanghai during a visit to China.[[415]](#cite_note-:05-423)

   * Spanish: [[saɱ fɾanˈsisko]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), lit. '[Saint Francis](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi")'
2. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** Construction of the Pacific Railroad was partially (albeit reluctantly) funded by the [City and County of San Francisco Pacific Railroad Bond issue](/wiki/File:Pacific_RR_Bond_SF_1865.jpeg "File:Pacific RR Bond SF 1865.jpeg") under the provisions of *"An Act to Authorize the Board of Supervisors of the City and County of San Francisco to take and subscribe One Million Dollars to the Capital Stock of the Western Pacific Rail Road Company and the Central Pacific Rail Road Company of California and to provide for the payment of the same and other matters relating thereto."* approved on April 22, 1863, as amended by §5 of the *"Compromise Act of 1864"* approved on April 4, 1864. The bond issue was objected to by the Mayor and the Board of Supervisors, however, and they were not delivered to the WPRR and CPRR until 1865 after Writs of Mandamus ordering such were issued by the Supreme Court of the State of California in 1864 (*"The People of the State of California on the relation of the Central Pacific Railroad Company vs. Henry P. Coon, Mayor; Henry M. Hale, Auditor; and Joseph S. Paxson, Treasurer, of the City and County of San Francisco"* 25 Cal 635) and 1865 (*"The People ex rel The Central Pacific Railroad Company of California vs. The Board of Supervisors of the City and County of San Francisco, and Wilhelm Lowey, Clerk"* 27 Cal 655)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-116)** "Another positive trend for the future is San Francisco's highly entrepreneurial, flexible and innovative economy...San Francisco's very high reliance on small business and self-employment is typical of other dynamic, fast-growing, high-technology areas across the country."[[112]](#cite_note-ega06a-115)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-149)** Station currently at the [United States Mint](/wiki/San_Francisco_Mint#Current_building "San Francisco Mint") building[[144]](#cite_note-148)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
5. **[^](#cite_ref-156)** The coordinates of the station are [37°46′14″N 122°25′37″W﻿ / ﻿37.7706°N 122.4269°W﻿ / 37.7706; -122.4269](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=San_Francisco&params=37.7706_N_122.4269_W_type:landmark_region:US-CA). Precipitation, high temperature, low temperature, snow, and snow depth records date from October 1, 1849; June 1, 1874; January 1, 1875; January 1, 1876; and January 1, 1922; respectively.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-157)** Mean monthly maxima and minima (i.e. the expected highest and lowest temperature readings at any point during the year or given month) calculated based on data at said location from 1991 to 2020.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-180)** Those not born in the 50 states or D.C., excluding California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas before 1850.
